> Based on the NRRD specs, it shows that none is possible for rows of the matrices.

Right. What I meant is: when `none` *is present*, the corresponding axis does not have a spatial interpretation. So filling in `none` -> `(NaN, Nan, Nan)` seems like it could lead some some confusion (on the other hand, that's what the nrrd C library does). Prior versions of the code would just parse:

  `none (1, 0, 0), (0, 1, 0), (0, 0, 1)`
as
  `['none', ['1', '0', '0'], ['0', '1', '0'], ['0', '0', '1']]`

...which matches my own inclination to stay as close to source as possible. (the last vectors could of course be converted to float).

> Do you have any reason for why the dtype argument needs to be present in the function?

No -- maybe it was unclear from the title, but this PR *removes* `dtype=` in the one call where it is currently used (which raises an error).

I had considered using mutex groups, but I found the usage turns out like this:

```
usage: backend.py [-h] [-x GDB_CMD_FILE] [-g GDB] [--rr] [-p PORT]
                  [--host HOST] [-r] [--auth-file AUTH_FILE] [--user USER]
                  [--password PASSWORD] [--key KEY] [--cert CERT]
                  [--license LICENSE] [--project PROJECT] [-v]
                  [--hide_gdbgui_upgrades] [-n] [-b BROWSER] [--debug]
                  [--args ...]
                  [cmd]

A server that provides a graphical user interface to the gnu debugger (gdb).
https://github.com/cs01/gdbgui

positional arguments:
  cmd                   Name of the binary to run in gdb. To pass flags to the
                        binary, use --args instead. Example: gdbgui ./mybinary
                        [gdbgui-args...]

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  --args ...            All remaining args are taken as the binary and
                        arguments to run in gdb (as with gdb --args). Example:
                        gdbgui [...] --args ./mybinary myarg -flag1 -flag2
gdb commands:
[...]
```

In which case `--args` or `cmd` are not shown under `gdb commands` section; nor are they shown with a mutex pipe as I would expect (i.e. `[--args ...|cmd]`). The latter is likely because `cmd` is a positional argument whereas `--args` is an optional argument. I believe it is for the same reason that `--args` shows up before `cmd` in the list, even though `--args` is set to `argparse.REMAINDER`. (That could be considered a deficiency in argparse because `argparse.REMAINDER` options should always occur last, even after positional arguments... oh well.)

However, it does accomplish placing both `cmd` and `--args` at the top of the usage like we want (I agree that makes sense) and it saves us having to check the mutex condition ourselves.

I also realized I could fix 'cmd' from str->list using `type=`.

While the refresh request is in "pending" state, the exception trace is printed.
It s not a big problem if there is only one request, since flower would die after that anyway.

Problem arise when there are multiple parallel request doing "refresh", in "pending" state at the same time... It ~looks like~ flower is trying to get information from the worker once per request (instead of only one time), and this delays the moment when flower will be shutdown... To the point where it can prevent a restart (socket still in use)...

I was hitting this issue when my webapp was querying (through API) the state of workers every 2 seconds (from one client), without caring about the return status (it didnt wait for a result before asking again). And requests where piling up, and flower was overloaded by refresh request and wouldn't shutdown.

I now make sure one client can only send a request when a response has been received, so it s not blocking for me at the moment. But it will become a problem again when I start having multiple clients...

I need to refresh often, because in my environment, workers can go up and down very quickly. I am working with robots, and they can easily : 
- go out of wifi range
- get a long going task that update it s state very often
- even be turned off and back on only 3 weeks later.
So I need refreshed status very often (online/offline, task state, etc.) via the REST API.

Hi Janusz,

thanks for your comments.

# 1. Timeout
I created pull request #16. 

# 2. Async

> We need a way to handle multiple DBus messages at once, that's obvious.

Not only multiple DBus messages, but any other event you can imagine. The DBus interface might be an ancillary control interface.

> However, I don't think that going async in this way is a good idea. We get multiple ways to do everything, and the faster one is the hideous-looking one.

I agree, classical async programming is hideous. But it is powerful, resource efficient and gives you ful control over the behaviour of your program.

> And the main pydbus's goal is to enable writing perfect code. A better-looking solution would be to return futures from methods that want to be executed asynchronously. This way, we don't need to pass them any special arguments - they simply return a concurrent.future object, and we subscribe to it's result with add_done_callback.

Here I can't agree with you. First of all, an exported method might need to get some attributes of the invocation like the sender, i.e. for authorization (see #13). This requirement is completely independent from synchronous, asynchronous or concurrent execution. Returning a concurrent.future is no solution for this requirement. Using an argument is a quite natural way to pass this information. (Other options: a API-function, that returns the information.)

Secondly the requirement to process many events at once has two fundamental solutions: 

- either sequential asynchronous execution
- or concurrent synchronous execution. 

Of these the asynchronous (first) one is more general. If you have an asynchronous API, it is trivial to derive a synchronous one, but you can't create an asynchronous API from a synchronous one (without threads, greenlets or tasklets or some other forms of concurrent execution, which might not be available). Therefore a general purpose DBus library should support asynchronous DBus programming at its lowest level. Of course this shouldn't be the only way to use pydbus. Additional support of concurrent.futures is a good idea.

The Python module concurrent.futures is a generic implementation of the concurrent synchronous approach (see PEP 3148). It strongly recommends, that you use a subclass of concurrent.futures.Executor to create a concurrent.futures.Future object. This is a fairly severe limitation. In many situations you don't want to use concurrent.futures:

- hard real time systems
- embedded systems without threads
- classical GLib/GObject/Gtk based applications, that run single threaded and use the good old GLib.MainLoop() and async-functions. Because pydbus is also a GLib/Gio based application, I'm strongly convinced, that pydbus should play nice in such an application. 

> However, the best solution would be to use greenlets/gevent - and run all the methods in new greenlets, so that developers would magically get the performance of async handlers without writing the hideous code.

I'm one of the few remaining maintainers of Stackless Python and I know the power and the limitations of tasklets or greenlets very well. It is really cool, but it is not mainstream and has it's own problems.

> I don't know how much work is needed to make pydbus gevent-compatible (which includes making all the client functions non-blocking (in the gevent's sense)), however it would be quite useful. It would also seamlessly enable using pydbus in WSGI applications served with gunicorn/uwsgi/any other python server relying on green threads. If you're interested in doing some research in that field, and possibly in porting pydbus to gevent - great! If not - I guess I'll do it next month.

From my Stackless experience: if you have a good asynchronous or otherwise non-blocking API as a starting point it is usually fairly simple to build a non-blocking API in the sense of Stackless. It should be similar for gevent. 
But unfortunately I don't have much spare time this summer. Currently I prepare a talk about new-style sd-daemons at http://www.tuebix.org/ and then I have to renovate my dwelling. The pydbus patch is for the tuebix talk. If we could agree on a solution before April 11th, I could include Polkit authorization in my presentation. Otherwise I'll fall back to SystemBus policies.

Would you be OK with an updated pull request, that adds support for concurrent futures but keeps the asynchronous coding as an option?

# 3. Polkit

Here I'm fine with your suggestions. I'll rework the implementation as soon as we agree on the async question.

I know you're busy, I've been banging my head against this for several weeks and in the meantime have found about a dozen similarly affected people.

The bug you linked to isn't yours per se, but seems very related to https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=656325, and I'm wondering how to proceed.  The bug you linked to was the last blocker.

The frustrating thing (not your fault) is that I've been told "use gdbus" but I have not managed to piece together a working python dbus server afflicted by some bug in some way.  This comment from the previous thread is especially frustrating after being told to "use gdbus" myself:

> Interesting fact: GDBus was never _intended_ to be bindable - the idea was always that languages would provide their own D-Bus bindings based on libdbus-1. That was the thinking at the time GDBus was designed. And the main reason that GVariant was used (as GVariant wasn't designed to be bound either, IIRC.. at least not without a lot of work.) It's more an accident that mostly everything is easily bindable..

Again, not your fault, but I wanted to link these things together in some way, any further details/folk-lore you can add would be welcome.  The especially annoying thing is that some of these issues date to 2011, I guess that's when the focus from desktop to mobile really really shifted.

My analysis: in general, the new-style gtk3/Gio/Glib bindings were heavily designed around desktop use, while interest shifted to mobile and internet of things.  In particular, the only reason I can't use the older python-dbus is because the mainloop will coalesce and duplicate events if you attempt to create new loops in temporarily lived background threads. Meanwhle subclassing from `Gtk.Application` works fantastic unless you didn't want or need a GUI, and really just wanted dbus/webosckets/IPC.

Other considered sources, for note taking:
* https://github.com/lvht/hello-dbus/issues/1
* https://zignar.net/2014/09/08/getting-started-with-dbus-python-systemd/
* https://github.com/nathan-hoad/gbulb/blob/master/gbulb/glib_events.py

Currently, I have no idea when the bug fixes mentioned above are likely to be shipped by distros; in the meantime, python segfaults or errors randomly when trying test scripts using register_object.

Anyway, thanks again for the wonderful library.  If you still have any connection with some of the other authors involved, I'd love to understand the next needed steps better.

Did a bit of experimentation with this and on Windows, sure enough, about 15 seconds.  On my Macbook, it ran in about 0.02 seconds.   So, there is definitely some kind of platform-specific performance issue involved there.   However, I also looked closely at the Curio code and found that sleep(0) makes a task go through the entire sleep cycle anyways.   I modified it to special case 0 as a fast-reschedule and that made the problem go away.   I pushed this change yesterday. Try it and see what happens in your application.

Might worth some amount of investigation to see what's causing that massive slowdown on Windows.  There's really not much going on in the code other than calls to time_monotonic() and select().   I'll investigate further when I get a chance.

I'm somewhat less concerned with scheduling of locks/queues like this because I can't really envision a real-world situation where a program would just be spinning forever on a lock and never performing any I/O (a program like that would end up be 100% cpu-bound which is definitely not the kind of thing you'd want to run on Curio).     That said, I suppose the code could poll for I/O on rescheduling.   I don't know.  Open to thoughts.

I suppose the `run()` method could be restricted by a threading RLock or something.   To be honest, I haven't given it much thought---although part of the reason for the insular nature of the current `run()` method was to make it practically impossible for anything (especially threads) to externally interact with the kernel except via async/await.

I don’t want math on my `Quantity` instances to be polymorphic across the library. Full stop. If I put a `Quantity` into a calculation, I don’t want that type to change. We have a way in `pint` to make a `float`: `Quantity(f, ‘dimensionless’)`.

I come at this not from a unit purity point of view, but as someone who is implementing a large library of unit-aware calculations. Changing types when you don’t have to is an interoptibility nightmare; it’s doubly terrible when you try to teach using the library to scientists who are barely comfortable writing code.

Given that `pint` supports `cos`, `sin`, etc. and `exp` that would nominally return “floats” and do so using `’dimensionless’`, the sensible option IMO is to do the same from `x**Quantity` (i.e. `__rpow__`).

Here is the related pull request: #411

Cget never calls `git clone`, it just downloads the source archive. Here are some thing you can do:

1) Use the actual release distribution instead. For example, boost requires git modules, but you can download the complete source as part of the release, [here](https://dl.bintray.com/boostorg/release/1.64.0/source/). A library should provide source tarballs as part of the release, if they don't you should file a bug report.

2) Sometimes libraries use submodules for a poor dependency management. If this is the case, the library should look for the dependencies if the submodules are missing, which means you can have cget install the dependencies. If this is not the case, you should file a bug report.

Finally, if neither of those are options, you can clone or download the missing pieces in cmake. You can create a cmake file('build.cmake') which does something like this:

```cmake
# Clone the submodules manually
execute_process(COMMAND git clone -b master git@github.com:pfultz2/cget.git ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/cget)

# Include the original cmake file
include(${CGET_CMAKE_ORIGINAL_SOURCE_FILE})
```

And install the library with `cget install -X build.cmake <url>`. Or you can create a recipe to do this, see [here](https://github.com/pfultz2/cget-recipes/tree/master/recipes/curl) for a simple example.

Looking at the 3rd party package code that triggers the error, it boils down to this:

```
import six


class Meta(type):
    pass


class A(six.with_metaclass(Meta)):
    __metaclass__ = Meta
```

Removing the `__metaclass__` prevents the TypeError.

@mdshw5 @roryk my reading of https://www.sqlite.org/lang_analyze.html and https://www.sqlite.org/fileformat2.html#stat1tab is that the ANALYZE command gives *approximations* for optimizing queries and doesn't seem to need updating unless a lot of things change. I think it would not be time-efficient to trigger an ANALYZE after every update to the db (analagous to `__setitem__`).

Bulk updates are typically done with the `FeatureDB.update()` method, and index creation and ANALYZE are run at the end of that (in the `_finalize()` call of the creator object). ANALYZE can now be manually triggered using `FeatureDB.analyze()`. If you come up with other cases where it should be run, please let me know (prob via a separate issue).

Also when loading an existing db, if the `sqlite_stat1` table doesn't exist then you get a UserWarning with a suggestion to run `db.analyze()` to speed things up.

I didn't actually make any code changes because it wasn't clear that it was the correct thing to _always_ do. 

Instead, I manually edited the prototxt file to set the padding of the MaxPool layer to 1 (as it was in the Dlib model). The problem with that, however, is the output layer is now a different shape. Fortunately, It is simply a single row/col too big, so I inserted a CropLayer after the MaxPool layer to bring the output layer back to the correct size (this required fixing up the top/bottom pointers to the adjacent layers, of course).

After doing this, I was able to get the same encodings in Caffe as I got in Dlib.

I've included the relevent layers:

```
layer {
  name: "max_pool375"
  type: "Pooling"
  bottom: "relu376"
  top: "max_pool375"
  pooling_param {
    pool: MAX
    kernel_h: 3
    kernel_w: 3
    stride_h: 2
    stride_w: 2
    pad_h: 0
    pad_w: 0
  }
}
layer {
  name: "con373"
  type: "Convolution"
  bottom: "max_pool375"
  top: "con373"
  convolution_param {
    num_output: 64
    pad_h: 0
    pad_w: 0
    kernel_h: 1
    kernel_w: 1
    stride_h: 1
    stride_w: 1
  }
}
```

became:

```
layer {
  name: "max_pool375"
  type: "Pooling"
  bottom: "relu376"
  top: "max_pool375"
  pooling_param {

This seems like a good idea, I'm not entirely sure what the debug flag is even for if it's not for this.

That being said I've thought about migrating everything to the [brace style adapter pattern](https://stackoverflow.com/a/24683360/358960) which would then only format messages that are actually printed, (so if the loglevel is set to ERROR even info messages don't use much).

OTOH it's adding 2 levels of indirection (the style adapter and the BraceMessage class), so I figured it might very well be slower, so I went with YAGNI.

Additionally there's f-strings... which can't be made lazy (without lots of hacks), but format much faster (since the format string is parsed at compile time, among other reasons.). Also would have to drop support for python 3.5... which maybe isn't a big deal... but I'm not sure it would be worth it.

Though... comparing all these things (especially if you using something really exotic, like [macropy](https://github.com/lihaoyi/macropy)), might make a good blog post.

You can get select-like behaviour just by using the `asyncio` stdlib. `wait` is your friend here.

But a nice unix-y `select()` would be useful.

Using the approach from the linked page is probably the wrong way to go about it though.
Threading-based channels have issues that we don't need to think about.
A select for `Channel` (which in fact would work for any list of futures) would be much much simpler.

I think it would be fairly easy to build on top of `asyncio.wait()`.
I'm not a 100% sure on how to handle channels closing,
and if using wait and then cancelling the `pending` ones when a `done` future is returned, or
if it would make more sense to let the select be a more specialised class that holds state and is only for Channels. 

One option would be to do something like:
```python

from aiochannel import aioselect, Channel, ChannelClosed

c1 = Channel()
c2 = Channel()
c3 = Channel()
# ...
async def main():
    channels = [c1, c2, c3]
    while len(channels) > 0:
        try:
            (channel, value) = await aioselect(channels, timeout=5.0)
        except ChannelClosed:
            # one of the channels have been closed
            channels = filter(lambda c: not c.is_closed(), channels)
        do_work(channel, value)
    # all channels closed here (or timeout)
```

alternatively, a `Channel`-only select could handle close events internally
```python

from aiochannel import Channel, ChannelClosed

c1 = Channel()
c2 = Channel()
c3 = Channel()

# ...

async def main():
    channels = [c1, c2, c3]
    # The timeout is for the entire block
    async with Channel.select(channels, timeout=5.0) as selector:
        while not selector.closed(): # a selector will close when all channels are closed
            (channel, value) = await selector(timeout=1.0) # timeout for individual items
            do_work(channel, value)
    # all channels are closed here (or timeout)
```

Both of the above examples should probably thow a `TimeoutError` on timeout.

The first example doesn't really save much code and is error-prone. It will probably also perform worse then example two, where we can hide a bunch of cleverness in the async context manager.

So I think the second approach would be a good candidate.
Thoughts?

Looks like it's `pysam`. What's happening is that `y = pybedtools.BedTool("1000genomes-example.vcf.gz")` triggers a call to `pybedtools.helpers.isBAM`, which in turn checks to see if it's BGZIPed. It correctly identifies that it's BGZIPed, and then tries to differentiate between an arbitrary BGZIP and an actual BAM file. The problem is that I had been doing this by catching the ValueError raised when trying to open the file with `pysam.Samfile`, but this is what causes the segfault.

E.g., 

```python
pysam.Samfile("1000genomes-example.vcf.gz")
# segfaults
```

The solution is to use the BAM magic number to detect if it's BAM or not, but I must not be reading the SAM spec correctly since I'm unable to extract it properly using `struct.unpack_from`. Will fix this shortly.

This is by design, and is standard behavior for AST visiting functions. It mimics Python's own AST module (described in https://docs.python.org/dev/library/ast.html?highlight=ast#ast.NodeVisitor)

If you override `visit_FOO`, it's assumed that you have some ideas of custom processing of `FOO`. You can always propagate visitation to the children of `FOO` by invoking `self.generic_visit(node)` from the visitor.

You got it right. I've already managed to find a couple of other errors related to reference counting in this file (but it's possible that they are harmless because they are never reached) e.g.:
https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/c4c6e22ca4dad687d2a8505f9e0207d8ecd64358/psutil/_psutil_osx.c#L144-L149

You should not assign `Py_None` value without INCREFing it. I'd replace that assignment with:
```
py_name = Py_BuildValue("");
```

Regarding the general fix, I also thought about setting pointer to NULL after being DECREFed. It's not required in most cases but it will easily prevent us from such errors (assuming that Py_XDECREF is used in error handling section). Additionally, it's not a very uncommon style in C programming (setting pointer to NULL once it's freed). Such defensive programming makes debugging dangling pointer bugs much easier - accessing NULL pointer will crash immediately on most platforms instead of reading/overwriting some random memory and leaving your app in undefined state.

> No, we want it to propagate. Otherwise it would just return an incorrect result (an empty list).

That's not entirely true. You can get EPERM error just for a part of file descriptors so the result can still have some entries. That's why I suggested omitting this error instead of raising AccessDenied exception.

When an overflow happens, we have to run a full filesystem scan in a separate process to see what changes we missed. We also have to restart the pyinotify-based processes because we're not sure if we missed watch changes. So in our case we just want to terminate all the pyinotify-based python. And then start it fresh and run cleanup processes.

Thanks for mentioning the other special event types, that does make a good case for a callback.

Yes, if we make the inotify buffer large enough, everything is fine until the buffer is no longer large enough. Our filesystems are growing continuously and we've already been bit by overflow. There must be some fundamental limit based on how fast changes can reach the OS, but I'm not sure if we've found it. Our current limit is fs.inotify.max_queued_events=3000000 after we overflowed 1,000,000.

It sounds like it would be better for you to decide exactly how to implement the general solution. It's not urgent; we have the buffer set big enough for now and I can just patch our local copy with simpler protection if it will take you an extended time. If you want to outline the desired approach in more detail I'd be happy to help.

The top priority stats you suggest are perfect.  A couple questions about them:

```
GPU Nvidia 2GB   <-- Question #1
Processor: 40%
Memory:    75%
IO:   1.1 GB/s   <-- Question #2
```

1.  Should we identify the GPU by its size (EX: 2GB), device id, UUID, or model (EX: GTX 560 Ti)

   I prefer identifying by size, because id and UUID are not informative or human readable, and model could be a very long string.

2. Memory bandwidth is an important stat, but is [not currently supported on virtualized GPU environments](http://docs.nvidia.com/deploy/nvml-api/group__nvmlDeviceQueries.html#group__nvmlDeviceQueries_1gd86f1c74f81b5ddfaa6cb81b51030c72), like EC2.  In those cases, should we not show that line, or just show `N/A` for the value?

-----------

For multiple GPUs, we could follow the standard for multiple CPUs, where the % utilization is combined.  People are used to that for CPU, but it might be confusing for memory.  EX:  Say we have a 2x GPU setup, where both are at 90% processor usage, and 75% memory usage:

```
2x GPU Nvidia 2GB
Processor: 180%   <-- Combined, just like the top command
Memory:    150%  <--- Combined.  Confusing?
IO:   2.9 GB/s   <-- Also combined, if available.
```

What do you think about combining stats like that?   The alternative would be to show average stats on each line, or to have a 4 line GPU section for each card.

I have been thinking about something to be safe when using multiple algebra in the same process some of which may be using some of the same types. Here is the problem:
This new design allow subclassing but at the same time uses class attributes added at the time the algebra is constructed. This means that you could create a first algebra with a custom OR and another algebra with another custom OR: the attribute types of AND/NOT for OR on the first algebra would be overwritten with the type of OR in the second algebra. Which would lead to byzantine behaviour.
As a solution and quite simply, the *_class provided in the BooleanAlgebra constructor could be dynamically subclassed in the initializer, using the same names as their base class. And the cyclic/cross-referencing attributes only added to these dynamic subclasses.
As a result, multiple algebra could be created without clashing since the classes used in an algebra would always be new and privately created sub types. Essentially  this would make the BooleanAlgebra a type factory. Some sort of meta meta class? but still allowing subclassing all elements and operations of the algebra for customization.
I will take a crack at this and add additional tests.

After a bit of search, it appears that indeed Python assumes that all text files are 1252 encoded under windows (which is a sad assumption, but that is another story). So it's not the minifier that makes that assumption. However, strangely enough, the minifier seems to be blindly converting the 1252 text file into utf8 somewhere in the process. 

To arrive to that conclusion, I wrote the following simple Python script (readwrite.py) which simply reads a text file and outputs its content into a new one:

```
import sys
with open(sys.argv[1]) as file_in : 
    with open(sys.argv[2], "w") as file_out :
        file_out.write(file_in.read())
```

When I run that script under windows over either an UTF-8 source, or an 1252 source, it outputs the same file in the same format, which is exactly what should be expected. In fact, for the UTF-8 file, Python assumes the 1252 encoding, and so accented characters are assumed to be two different characters, which are then written as is to the output, and so the output is still perfectly valid UTF-8. However, if I run the minifier like this `pyminifier -o out.py --nominify in_utf8.py` which simply copies the file, then the accented characters are not handled correctly (they get converted in the process somewhere).

INSERT BULK and the corresponding SqlClient feature is specific to SQL Server; while pyODBC does have some DB-specific code, that is all accomplished through what is otherwise the standard ODBC interface. I.e. pyODBC uses the driver manager to load an ODBC driver, and then talks to the driver through the DM. Using the BCP API (which is essentially what you're asking?) is very different, in that it involves explicitly loading and calling specific functions exported from the msodbcsql ODBC driver itself. This does not seem to be something pyODBC can easily accommodate.

What version of unixODBC are you using? You can try the latest [here](https://github.com/lurcher/unixODBC) to get a more informative trace.

tbh I haven't surveyed many DAWs in this regard, and you're probably right that there's no established convention. I've been working primarily with Logic Pro, where if two midi notes overlap in time/pitch they both just get synthesized (both the offset of the current note and the onset of the new note), as if they were just notes with different pitches. I found this functionality very useful, since being aware of every note onset was of the utmost importance when creating manual annotations. It also so happens that some of the midi files I got from you already contained this type of overlap, and if it weren't for this functionality in Logic I may have missed the presence of these notes altogether.

This is what they say in the [Nyquist docs](http://www.cs.cmu.edu/~rbd/doc/nyquist/part12.html):
> Be aware that overlapping notes on the same pitch can be a problem for some synthesizers. The following example illustrates this potential problem:
```
!TEMPO 60
C Q #160   * starts at time 0,   ends at 1.6 sec
D I        * starts at time 1,   ends at 1.8 sec
C Q        * starts at time 1.5, ends at 3.1 sec?
```
>At one beat per second (tempo 60), these three notes will start at times 0, 1, and 1.5 seconds, respectively. Since these notes have an articulation of 160, each will be on 160% of its nominal duration, so the first note (C) will remain on until 1.6 seconds. But the third note (another C) will start at time 1.5 seconds. Thus, the second C will be started before the first one ends. Depending on the synthesizer, this may cancel the first C or play a second C in unison. In either case, a note-off message will be sent at time 1.6 seconds. If this cancels the second C, its actual duration will be 0.1 rather than 1.6 seconds as intended. A final note-off will be sent at time 3.1 seconds.

Despite the lack of convention, my intuition says that the synthesis should match the concept of "note" as closely as possible, i.e. if there's a note between 1-3s and another between 2-4s, both should be audible, despite the overlap. The fact that the second note might get cancelled due to the note-off message of the first note feels more like an artifact of the midi protocol than a desired functionality. But again, that's just my personal preference.

```python
    def new_username(self, template: Optional[str] = None) -> str:
        name = self.random.choice(USERNAMES)
        date = str(self.random.randint(1800, 2070))

        templates = ('U_d', 'U.d', 'U-d', 'ld', 'l-d', 'Ud',
                     'l.d', 'l_d')

        if template is None:
            template = self.random.choice(templates)
        elif template not in templates:
            raise KeyError()

        templatez = {
            # UppercaseDate
            'Ud': lambda: name.capitalize() + date,
            # Uppercase.Date
            'U.d': lambda: name.capitalize() + '.' + date,
            # lowercaseDate
            'ld': lambda: name + date,
            # Uppercase-date
            'U-d': lambda: name.title() + '-' + date,
            # Uppercase_date
            'U_d': lambda: name.title() + '_' + date,
            # lowercase-date
            'l-d': lambda: name + '-' + date,
            # lowercase_date
            'l_d': lambda: name + '_' + date,
            # lowercase.date
            'l.d': lambda: name + '.' + date,
        }

        return templatez[template]()
```

Yeah, this needs better support. Currently recursive data like this is a real pain to do in Hypothesis. I've got some ideas germinating for how to make it better, but they're not there yet.

In the mean time, the best thing to do for this is probably to use the stateful testing support. You can basically define rules to build your data from smaller types (which can be recursive).

You can also use the under documented and slightly unofficial NAryTree strategy and map over that: https://github.com/DRMacIver/hypothesis/blob/master/src/hypothesis/searchstrategy/narytree.py

Well, the eventual solution to this is [Conjecture](http://github.com/DRMacIver/conjecture).

In the meantime I think you're going to find it much easier to test C code from Python using [CFFI](https://cffi.readthedocs.org/en/latest/) rather than trying to wrap Hypothesis into a usable C API. The API you're describing wouldn't be very hard to implement on top of the Hypothesis internal API, but I suspect that it's the easy part of what you want here.

The sorting is done to be able to process the data block using a numpy recarray

The channels are first ordered ascending (see method __lt__ of Channel class). If the channel's start bit is lower then the channel is "smaller", and if they have the same start bit then the one with a higher bit count is "smaller".

The idea is that normally, in case of overlapping channels, you should be able to extract a channel from it's parent, the parent being the "smaller" channel.

For simple data groups (no overlapping channels; all channels properly byte aligned and standard C type size) all channels will be their own parents.

A not byte aligned channel is flagged if it partially overlaps with the current parent but it also extends a bit to higher bit position in the record
```
        first parent  second                    current parent
bit0|-----------------|--------------------| ....   |----------------------|
                                                            |------------------------|  not byte aligned channel
                                                        |----------------|                       this is not flagged since it can be extracted from the parent
```
Parents are always 8byte multiples, and are the fields of the recarray that is used to process the samples.

This algorithm is not perfect but 99% of the time it works OK and all channels have a parent, and in case of more exotic bit size channels there should not be so many channels without a parent.

Following our discussion on IRC: I think processing in electrum-server is expensive because spent histories are sorted by the height of the incoming transaction. If we change the chronology and use the height of the outgoing (spending) transaction instead, then adding new transaction pairs becomes a append-only operation. I believe that this database structure would actually be lighter than what ElectrumX is currently doing, because it would remove the redundancy between UTXO and histories.

Ansible currently doesn't gather infomation about installed software. The easiest thing to do would be to create a `/etc/ansible/facts.d` directory on each server you manage with ansible. Then you put JSON files in there with the list of installed software on that system.

Ansible-cmdb will then incoorporate those facts under the "Host local facts" section of each host.

Hi, @dbobak.  Thanks for this excellent suggestion.  I have been thinking on it for a few days.  LaTeX supports "clever referencing" through the `cleveref` package.  To implement it for other output formats I just need to work out what `cleveref` is doing.

The question is how to best enable this behaviour.

It seems reasonable to add a metadata field to signal clever referencing; e.g., `-M cref` in the call to pandoc.  It is probably also necessary to have control on a per-reference class basis; e.g., `-M eqnos-cref`.  Each of these could be optionally set to `on` or `off`.

As you have pointed out, there needs to be some way to turn off clever referencing.  The use case is markdown text like `@fig:one and @fig:two` that would be transformed to "Figure 1 and Figure 2".  We need to turn off clever referencing in order to manually generate the text "Figures 1 and 2".  (Note: I know the `cleveref` package support grouping references, but I don't presently have a markdown spec that describes such behaviour.  Let's take it one step at a time.)

Using markdown like `-@fig:one` to disable clever referencing is well outside of the syntax developed in [Pandoc Issue #813](https://github.com/jgm/pandoc/issues/813).  It doesn't seem likely to me that pandoc would adopt it.  Perhaps we can come up with something better.

Pandoc is increasingly using attributes.  Can we use them instead?  e.g., something like `@fig:one{cref=off}`.  It isn't quite as economical, but it is more consistent with pandoc's direction and the meaning is clear.

Thoughts?

Glyph talked me down from this. I'm going to stick with a single PyPI distribution (`pip install magic-wormhole`), which will provide both blocking and Twisted flavors. The import names are still in flux, but I'm leaning towards `from wormhole import wormhole` which is a function that you call with a `reactor=` argument to get Twisted-style, and without a reactor for blocking-style).

The downside is the dependencies: no matter which flavor you want, you're going to have to install Twisted. But you don't need to know anything about Twisted to use the blocking flavor, so the only cost is a bit of disk space and download time.

Glyph suggested that I use [crochet](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/crochet) to provide the blocking API on top of a Twisted backend, which would 1: provide feature-parity between the two flavors (bringing Transit back to blocking code), and 2: remove the dependency on `requests` that doesn't help Twisted-flavor users. So that's in the works.

I've also split the server-management tool into a separate entrypoint script, `wormhole-server`, so that plain `wormhole` script is a bit simpler (although really the only visible consequence is the `--help` output is one line shorter, so I may merge them back together again).

Good summaries, sorry if I'm being overly verbose.

> libtcodpy provides binaries for Windows. tdl provides binaries for Windows and MacOS. (Both need to be compiled for Linux.)

> libtcodpy is smaller in size. tdl is larger, thanks to numpy modules.

> tdl allows you to serialize (pickle) everything except Image instances

These are correct.

> libtcodpy is written in C, and requires you to manage memory. tdl is written in Python and doesn't require you to handle memory.

Both libtcodpy and tdl use a combination of libtcod's C code, custom C code, and Python code.

What matters is that instead allocating memory in C and managing that C pointer from Python, tdl allocates the memory from Python and passes it to C.  tdl usually creates some numpy arrays, creates Python owned structs pointing to those arrays, then passes the pointers for those structs to C.

> libtcodpy is slightly more performant than tdl.

I'm calling out libtcodpy functions like `console_put_char_ex(con, x, y, c, fore, back)`, if you ever need to update every tile on a Console then Python will have to do a lot of type conversion.  Smaller games will be fine, but if you want your game to have a large window and a small tile size you'll quickly notice a hard limit on the number of calls this function can handle in a single frame.  BearLibTerm has a similar problem.

The way tdl fixes this specific issue is to have the character data and foreground/background colors as numpy arrays.  You can crunch your data in numpy and dump it directly into the Console buffers skipping Python's slow path entirely.  This method is magnitudes faster than calling the above function on each tile.

tdl also uses very aggressive optimization flags when compiling its C extension, including link-time-optimization.  You can expect that some of the larger C functions called from tdl will perform slightly better than the DLL's available from the official site.

If you get performance issues, it's usually because of Python.  In the old legacy tdl API I made the incredibly poor choice of using Python callbacks for everything.  It did make code really easy to write, but it tanked performance.  The libtcod-cffi API handles performance much better by discouraging the use of callbacks and encouraging the use of numpy arrays and batch operations.

Admittedly, this seems like a complicated setup, but it makes some sense. I have two drives: a small SSD (`/`) and a large HDD (`/HDD`). Hence, the quick SDD contains `/`, and includes most system and user directories. However, there is not enough room on the SDD for most of my working files, so my user has `~/HDD`, which points to the HDD (`/HDD/protist`). I then also link `~/.local/share/Trash` to the HDD (`/HDD/protist/.SDDsymlinks/home/protist/.local/share/Trash`). This is because most of the files that I trash would originally come from the HDD (`~/HDD`). This means trashed items are only moved, not copied to another volume. Also, this means that I don't waste space on the SDD with files scheduled to be deleted.

Hence, the path you enquired about is at its literal path.
```
$ readlink -f /HDD/protist/.SDDsymlinks/home/protist/.local/share/Trash
/HDD/protist/.SDDsymlinks/home/protist/.local/share/Trash
```

Not sure if this is your issue, but we hit something similar when trying to load signals that had references to shop models.  They were tripping up on the deferred models.  The solution was to move the signals import into the ready() method of AppConfig like so:

```
class SessionCoreConfig(AppConfig):
    name = 'session_core'
    verbose_name = "session_core"

    def ready(self):
        import session_core.signals  # noqa

```

You can replace both the python and the node interfaces if you need to either pass data in a particular format, or access some particular functionality.

As @TheoRyley said, you'll probably need to replace the node server with something that matches your needs. If you need to also pass extra data from the python side (request headers, session data, etc), you can either define a `renderer` kwarg - or roll your own pipeline - to pass data in whatever format you need.

from exchangelib.folders import ArchiveInbox

@SalahAdDin I've modified the `handler_tcp.py` file to encode the string. You can actually copy and paste this block into your file

```
from logging.handlers import DatagramHandler, SocketHandler
from logstash import formatter


# Derive from object to force a new-style class and thus allow super() to work
# on Python 2.6
class TCPLogstashHandler(SocketHandler, object):
    """Python logging handler for Logstash. Sends events over TCP.
    :param host: The host of the logstash server.
    :param port: The port of the logstash server (default 5959).
    :param message_type: The type of the message (default logstash).
    :param fqdn; Indicates whether to show fully qualified domain name or not (default False).
    :param version: version of logstash event schema (default is 0).
    :param tags: list of tags for a logger (default is None).
    """

    def __init__(self, host, port=5959, message_type='logstash', tags=None, fqdn=False, version=0):
        super(TCPLogstashHandler, self).__init__(host, port)
        if version == 1:
            self.formatter = formatter.LogstashFormatterVersion1(message_type, tags, fqdn)
        else:
            self.formatter = formatter.LogstashFormatterVersion0(message_type, tags, fqdn)

    def makePickle(self, record):
        return str.encode(self.formatter.format(record)) + b'\n'
```

The "init" while de-registering is correct. The Process is as follows:

pyhomematic connects to CCU with init and some identifier (_homeassistant_ in HA, _pyhomematic_ by default). The identifier later always is referenced as _interface_id_. The reason is, that multiple clients may connect to the CCUs XML-RPC, and this identifier ensures, that the clients can validate that they are the correct recipient.
When shutting down pyhomematic, it has to tell the CCU that it can stop pushing data for the respective client. This is done by using the init-method without an _interface_id_. From the initial init the CCU knows the IP + identifier of the client. Leaving out the identifier when init'ing again is enough for the CCU to know that this is acutally a de-register for the IP that is calling the init-method.

Currently we actually don't verify the _interface_id_ when handeling messages we receive. I've created an issue for this.

But I'm uncertain if that really is the cause for this issue. It might be. If you have multiple HAs running (each idenfiying themselves as _homeassistant_ to the CCU), shutting down one of them may break the connection of the other HA using the some identifier, which would prove that the second init is doing what it is supposed to do.

So, do you have multiple HAs running while testing?

Thank you for the detail!

Case-insensitive comparisons of user input are a common thing to do, sure. This library could've been built from the ground up to facilitate that, discarding the original input's case. But given its history, preserving case, it can't assume your use case (and only in 1 property). I think the most common remedy is your case is well known and compact: `str.lower` all the things you're comparing.

> We could go for `registered_domain_ex`, but that doesn't look too good.

Right. Another property increases API surface area. It's gotta be compelling to do so. Any suffix to distinguish the lowercased version from the preserved-case version will either be unclear, or more verbose than calling `str.lower` yourself.

So, history + familiar remedy + short remedy, I'll decline this.

full_class_path = config.registry.settings.get('service.my_service.class', None)

Let me repeat, that the problem with adding a set of options for each potential use case is that it eventually leads to bloated code that becomes near impossible to support in a backwards-compatible manner. I'm adding percentages today, tomorrow someone asks for special parameters for shifting circles around, next month we think we need a special parameter for capitalizing titles, etc. You could disagree with me, but I feel that a good function should provide the smallest possible functionality that makes sense together. Anything else, if it is unrelated to the point of being packageable into a separate abstraction, be it a module/function or a package, should be packaged as a separate abstraction.

As I noted, I would agree, that "number_formatter" is on the edge of something which might be present in the parameter list of venn. However, I still did not add it because, firstly I am using this library actively and so far never had the need, secondly, there do not seem to be much activity in this ticket either, thirdly, there is a simple way to implement it as a separate function, and, as I mentioned above, in my world this is a very strong indicator in favor of not rushing to integrate it into the function.

Namely, at the moment, setting any format for your labels is as simple as:

    def reformat_labels_to_my_taste(v):
        for sl in v.subset_labels:
            if sl is not None:
                sl.set_text('New: ' + sl.get_text())
            
    v = venn3([set([1,2,3]), set([2,3,4]), set([5,6,7])])
    reformat_labels_to_my_taste(v)

Which, among other things, gives you all the freedom to do whatever you like to the labels without having to worry that your code breaks in a year due to some random decision of mine regarding the changes to formatting or something (say, tonight I'm all in the mood of having label_format="%f", but in a month I feel that this was stupid and change it to label_format=lambda x: "%f" % x instead, or the like.

I do think that some standard diagram utility functions might be implemented somewhere in the `util` package eventually, though. But so far I'm delaying this as long as people are not actively demanding it nor offering something extremely constructive, due to the reasons described above.

I originally developed it for a project to be a drop-in replacement for AutoFields, without having to change the underlying DB field and migrate the data (and all the foreign keys as well), so I went with storing it as an integer and basing it off of IntegerField. I actually have a solution to this problem working in the `lookups` branch, but I need to hack on it a bit more to get it compatible with older version of Django (only works with 1.11 right now). It supports all of the lookups needed for the search functionality, but does so by basically making *exact*, *iexact*, *contains* and *icontains* all do the same thing, which is an *exact* search. *startswith* and *endswith* won't be supported, and *in* lookups will work, and right now even work with subqueries. But the lookups system has been changing a lot since it was introduced in 1.7, and I didn't realize how unstable it was, so I'll need to write a lot more specific code to support all the versions instead of being able to just tie into the right place like I'm doing now for 1.11.

Is there a problem when this `IOError` is raised? That should be a benign error, meaning that all other clients should continue to be connected and working normally.

The situation that you describe is abnormal, I think it is warranted to raise an error when this type of problem occurs.

If it helps, I fixed it by making the following changes:

- In engineio.socket.Socket._poll, after one packet is read I check if its an 'OPEN'. If so, I return that packet, else proceed to see if any other packets are available (which normal packs them together, but breaks on the OPEN). This delays the CONNECT correctly.
- In engineio.socket._websocket_handler I commented out 'self.queue.join() # flush the queue first', as there is no consumer this gets stuck.
- Lastly (but not related to this bug) I edited engineio.Server._ok so it checks the b64 flag and returns a content type of tex/plain if its set, else application/octet-stream. This fixes the dotnet socket.io client connecting.

And as I type this all out I realise they're all in engineio. Well hope it gives you some hints where you could look anyway.

    #'pymdownx.tilde',
    #'pymdownx.githubemoji',
    #'pymdownx.tasklist',
    'pymdownx.headeranchor',
    #'pymdownx.superfences'
]

md = markdown.Markdown(extensions=MARKDOWN_EXTENSIONS)

def run_instance(md, text):
    md.reset()
    return md.convert(text)

def run_fresh(text):
    return markdown.markdown(text, extensions=MARKDOWN_EXTENSIONS)

max_diff = 0
min_diff = 0
slower_count = 0
text = '# Hello World!\n\n' + 'f' * 100000
try:
    for x in range(0, 20000):
        start = time.clock()
        html = "test" + run_instance(md, text)
        delay = time.clock() - start

        start = time.clock()
        html = "test" + run_fresh(text)
        delay_fresh = time.clock() - start

        diff = delay - delay_fresh

        if diff > 0.0:
            slower_count += 1

        if diff > max_diff:
            max_diff = diff
            print("Using an instance is %.6fs slower than using markdown.markdown() (Text Length: %d)" % (diff, len(text)))

        if diff < min_diff:
            min_diff = diff
            print("Using an instance is %.6fs faster than using markdown.markdown() (Text Length: %d)" % (abs(diff), len(text)))

        if x % 500 == 0:
            print("Round %d finished." % x)

        text += '# Lorem' + '\n'
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("Instance was slower on %d rounds." % (slower_count))
```

Notice all we are doing is calling `md.reset()` before convert.  The assumption was that all you needed to do was call convert, but we need to clean up between runs by calling reset.  When calling reset, I see nothing wrong.

Sorry, but I don't like and don't want to follow PEP8.

As should be obvious from the code here, I kinda have my own weird and crappy style, and I've tried adopting PEP8 in the past, but all it led to is me abandoning the codebase, as it's too hard and inconvenient to work with (e.g. pulseaudio-mixer-cli projects, and some others) or refactoring back to my style (e.g. python-onedrive).

I guess many people might be fine with it though, likely the majority, so I think refactoring you did and stuff such as tests that you're planning to do is awesome and The Right Thing for a module useful for everybody, it's just that I'd rather maintain my own fork - way easier for me that way.

Furthermore, people asked for things like Python 2.6 and 3.x here a lot (iirc, check closed issues), which I'm also just "meh" about, and if you'll also be willing to implement these, I think it'll be fantastic.

------

So going forward, I think maintaining a separate pep8 and "best practices" fork is still the best option.

As I can't vouch for any fork (no offense), I'd rather not transfer PyPI project to fork that I don't know much about, as that can be unfair to any (probably super-rare) users of the project, especially if there are any significant changes planned there.

But given that the name of this project is kinda terrible anyway (as with most of my projects, blame lack of imagination), I think you can just come up with the better one and register that on PyPI instead, and I propose to link it at the top of the README with "FOR NEW PROJECTS USE THAT ONE INSTEAD" kind of message.

You can also send message to github support and they'll make your fork not to be considered such (i.e. no "forked from ..." at the top), which I think might be a good idea with a separate pypi project - to avoid confusion for new users as to which "fork" to pick, and some other upsides github-ui-wise.

Does that make any sense?

I think that rather than relying on the socket to govern the logic, you should track time directly. There are cases where a socket read could be interrupted without actually passing bytes back to the application. These are few and far between, but in the interests of getting the logic correct, I recommend that be taken into consideration.

Something like this perhaps, with `_last_octet_time` initialized when the socket connection is established (before the handshake):

```python
data = self._transport.read(self._heartbeat)
c_time = time.time()

if data is None:
    if self._heartbeat and (c_time-(self._last_octet_time or c_time)>2*self._heartbeat):
        self.close(reply_code=501,
                   reply_text='Heartbeats not received for %d seconds' % 2*self._heartbeat,
               class_id=0, method_id=0, disconnect=False)
        raise ConnectionClosed("connection is closed: %s : %s" %
                         (self._close_info['reply_code'],
                          self._close_info['reply_text']))
 
    return

self._last_octet_time = c_time
```

Unless you use the optional `x-nullable` support, the value should never be None. It's either what the caller passed, converted to the right type according to the spec (which might be an empty string, the number 0, the boolean value False and so on), or it will be None in the case the caller never passed an argument with that name. So providing a query parameter in the form of `intvar=&...` (i.e. an empty value for an integer argument) will fail request validation.

I have not looked at this in a long time.  IIRC, the underlying issue was that the "unrecognized arguments" error is raised by Django code, not by the django-extensions code.  FWIW, I have started doing things this way:

```
def do_real_work(foo, bar):
    print foo, bar

def run(*args):
    """
    usage:  python manage.py runscript myscript --script-args="--foo=XXX --bar=YYYY"

    example:  python manage.py runscript myscript --script-args="--foo=something --bar=3"
    """
    import shlex
    import argparse

    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--foo", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--bar", required=True)

    try:
        split_args = shlex.split(args[0])
    except IndexError:
        split_args = []

    args2 = parser.parse_args(split_args)
```

and then my invocation looks like

    python manage.py runscript myscript --traceback --script_args="--foo=something --bar=3"

For what it's worth, I think this is the code with the thread safety issue (from botocore/session.py):

```
    def get_component(self, name):
        if name in self._deferred:
            factory = self._deferred.pop(name)
            self._components[name] = factory()
        try:
            return self._components[name]
        except KeyError:
            raise ValueError("Unknown component: %s" % name)
```

If two threads evaluate the "name in" check to True, only one will successfully pop on the next line.  The other will fail.  Seems like putting a lock around this section would fix things.

In the meantime I'm putting this in my startup code under a lock:
```
boto3.setup_default_session()
boto3.DEFAULT_SESSION._session.get_component('data_loader')
boto3.DEFAULT_SESSION._session.get_component('event_emitter')
boto3.DEFAULT_SESSION._session.get_component('endpoint_resolver')
boto3.DEFAULT_SESSION._session.get_component('credential_provider')
```

The most straightforward way to inject a custom header would be to use the event system in botocore.  Something like:
```
def inject_header(params, **kwargs):
    params['headers']['Custom-Header'] = 'Some-Value'


session.events.register('before-call.s3', inject_header)
```

The above is saying that before every s3 call, the `inject_header` function will be called and passed the request dictionary (which contains a `headers` key you can add to).

I had a go at seeing what it would be like to implement this in the way you propose. It should be doable. The tricky thing is that the `IGallerySettings` interface is used for both the control panel and the gallery on the context. The enabled display types should only be settable in the control panel.

And there is a `__getattr__` override in the settings, which is always nice... It gets the value from the context and when it is not there it gets the value from the control panel and when the default has never been changed here, it takes the default from the interface. And with what we would add, this default would get its value from a new enabled display types vocabulary, which itself will have the current display types as default. I think... I ended up in a recursion error already, with a `defaultFactory` trying to get a value from a vocabulary, which has a default factory, etcetera. So it is fallback on fallback on fallback, which makes it a bit hard to keep it all in your head...

Having two fields already makes it easier to think about: so one for the enabled types, and one for the default type, instead of putting those two in a single ordered in/out widget.

So: implementation could be a challenge. But before we put more work into this: would this feature be acceptable?

> Raises an Exception (ex. ValueError) with a description of the reason why the string doesn't conform to the supported subset of ISO 8601

FYI I'm fine with that, but I'm also okay if we skip the exact reason if it benefits performance or simplifies the code.


> parse_datetime_unaware existed for the case where your input timestamp had tzinfo, but you wanted to ignore the tzinfo and therefore could save some cycles by not parsing the tzinfo characters.

It also exists for the case where your code doesn't (need to) deal with aware datetimes. People have different opinions on whether to use aware or naive datetimes, but e.g. http://lucumr.pocoo.org/2011/7/15/eppur-si-muove/ suggests that "internally always use offset naive datetime objects". Personally, I also prefer to use naive datetimes throughout the code (which are assumed to be in UTC), and most of my use cases actually use `parse_datetime_unaware`.


> > It could either ignore the timezone information

> This breaks the invariant of matching the entire string, as discussed above.

An idea is to still ensure that the string is valid ISO8601 in its entirety (i.e. you reject strings with an invalid time zone offset), but ignore the timezone information when returning the Python string (or add the offset to the naive datetime so it's always UTC). Would have to check what the actual performance penalty is.

> > or only accept timezone-less datetime strings
> parse_datetime can already handle both tz-aware and tz-naive timestamps without issue.

Just to be clear, would `parse_datetime` still always return a tz-aware datetime (even if there is no TZ information in the datetime string)?

Acutally, thinking about this some more, it's not easy to guarantee that the `SELECT *` is the exact same as the `SELECT a, b, ...`
- the column order in the sqlalchemy table object might be different from the actual order
- the table object might have an old version of the schema

I don't see an obvious way around those edge cases. (I'd rather not make another RPC for this optimization). As a workaround you should be able to do `select(['*'])` or preprocess the query yourself before sending it to the library.

@feluxe While I don’t necessarily strongly disagree with your points, this may not be the best venue to hold the discussion. I would suggest raising this issue on mailing lists like python-ideas to get more feedback.

Some background: Python has historically made a clear distinction between library and application dependencies (as mentioned several times previously). Pipfile has been developed to handle specifically the application part of the story, with Kenneth repeatedly emphasising Pipfile should be “the single source of truth” (this would never be truth if Pipfile is intended to use for library dependencies). There has been similar movements in lib-dep packaging to move away from old formats as well. The most significant one currently is the implementation of [setup.cfg as an alternative metadata provider to setup.py](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#configuring-setup-using-setup-cfg-files). There has been proposals to furthur improve the format with the [introduction of pyproject.toml](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/).

There is still time to try unifying the formats. With [PEP 390](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0390/) rejected and [426](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0426/) withdrawn, dependency specification is still not taken in the next-generation format, and Pipfile can still fill that role. But you’ll need to raise this issue in a more visible way to push the discussion toward the direction you want.

I don’t necessarily like the idea of mixing library and application dependency specifications, especially in Python, but that’s a whole other topic. I would very much love to see discussion around this issue, however, with more input from more knowledgable people, such as Nick and Donald.

Here's my pattern of thinking, after watching @kennethreitz's talk about Python for Humans, his talk on Pipenv, and seeing this on the Pipenv homepage:

> Pipenv is a tool that aims to bring the best of all packaging worlds (bundler, composer, npm, cargo, yarn, etc.) to the Python world. Windows is a first–class citizen, in our world.

npm is, in many ways, a very user-friendly package manager, especially around running tests, running scripts, and publishing packages. `npm test` and `npm publish` and `npm start` are all common commands.

For most python package developers right now, `python setup.py test` and `python setup.py sdist && python setup.py bdist_wheel --universal` are the closest we get to such simple commands.

`pipenv` is amazing, I want to see it become _the_ defacto way to work with python packages, and I think that means adding convenience methods for common patterns.

I'm 100% open to the idea that there is a better way to accomplish what I'm suggesting. Maybe the pipfile should describe a `test` script? Maybe there's a better name than `setuppy`?

But today, trying to bridge the world of packages relying heavily on `setup.py` while using pipenv for working with those packages means I'm constantly typing `pipenv run python setup.py {command}`, and after a few dozen times typing those ten extra characters, I start to wonder how much we're asking of package maintainers in using pipenv.

Hence, this attempt at a solution.

Sure, I can add some comments. I think the method I'm using now is sort of papering over some deeper issues, but those are hard to fix. The biggest problem I see is that in a `DistInfoDistribution` object, you have a dependency mapping that looks like this:

```python
{None: ['common_dep'],
 'my_extra': ['extra_dep; extra = "my_extra"']
}
```

The problem is that `my_extra` is doubled up. It's really saying "when using `my_extra`, `extra_dep` is required, but only when `my_extra` is being used." Unfortunately, by the time we get to `WorkingSet.resolve`, all we have is "`extra_dep` is required, but only when `my_extra` is being used", only we've forgotten about how we want to use `my_extra`, so it just skips `extra_dep`. Probably the best way to fix this would be to remove the redundancy in the dep map, but then I'd need to have ways to clone and mutate `Requirement` objects.

The easier way it just to let `WorkingSet.resolve` know that we want to use `my_extra` so that it can properly check for all its requirements.

@madisonb @j-bennet ah hah, I see what's going on now. It's all about shell quote escaping rules, of course! Here's the proof:

    $ echo -o my.conf="TEST"
    -o my.conf=TEST

Believe it or not, if you escape this *in your shell*, it'll do the right thing:

    $ echo -o my.conf=\"TEST\"
    -o my.con="TEST"

Which means, if you escape the quotes in your command-line invocation of `sparse`, this will also work (as a workaround). Clearly we need to put some more thought into this.

Ironically, this is only true if you actually want a string literal via configuration. That's because, for example, storm conf options that are specified with integer literals (like topology.max.spout.pending=1000) will **actually** get interpreted as integers when parsed by the Clojure/JVM interop layer with Storm. Very confusing, I admit.

I kinda think the best approach would be to view pixel coordinates (and shapes using them) as being defined by some header file in some way.

I started this approach by hijacking `SkyCoord` to represent pixel coordinates with custom frames [here](https://github.com/sargas/pyregion/blob/regions-submodule/pyregion/regions/frames/core.py), with examples [here](https://github.com/sargas/pyregion/blob/regions-submodule/pyregion/regions/tests/test_frames.py). Image<->Physical frame transformations could be added based on `pyregion`'s [physical coordinate code](https://github.com/astropy/pyregion/blob/master/pyregion/physical_coordinate.py)

This API [extends](https://github.com/sargas/pyregion/blob/regions-submodule/pyregion/regions/tests/test_transformations.py) to making a simple API for regions, where regions or shapes can be transformed simply as:
```
region_object.transform_to(frame_object)
```
where ```frame_object``` can be some sky coordinate system (e.g., ICRS) or pixel-based (i.e., physical, image, amplifier, etc).

This is a different approach then what `regions` is already using though, with separate `SkyRegion` and `PixelRegion` objects with exclusive functionality.

`id()` does not suffice because it is not repeatable for a given seed; i.e. we want the same random sample to be returned each time we seed with the same string. For example:

    >>> import hashlib
    >>> firstHash = hashlib.sha224('hello world').hexdigest()
    >>> secondHash = hashlib.sha224('hello world').hexdigest()
    >>> firstId = id('hello world')
    >>> secondId = id('hello world')
    >>> firstHash == secondHash
    True
    >>> firstId == secondId
    False

@rhyolight I think education about this is important. One look at this description for the ```mapPotential()``` method and you can see its obvious that this is not a "trivial" topic...
```Python
* If the potentialRadius is greater than or equal to the largest input
      dimension then each column connects to all of the inputs.
    * If the topology is one dimensional, the input space is divided up evenly
      among the columns and each column is centered over its share of the
      inputs.  If the potentialRadius is 5, then each column connects to the
      input it is centered above as well as the 5 inputs to the left of that
      input and the five inputs to the right of that input, wrapping around if
      wrapAround=True.
    * If the topology is two dimensional, the input space is again divided up
      evenly among the columns and each column is centered above its share of
      the inputs.  If the potentialRadius is 5, the column connects to a square
      that has 11 inputs on a side and is centered on the input that the column
      is centered above.
```

I think this makes sense. If the user only wants `TemporalAnomaly`, there is no reason to specify `predictedField`. 

The side effect of this is that predictions will no longer be available within anomaly model results. This may be acceptable, but there are many cases where you might want them in addition to anomaly scores and likelihoods. 

So for this to make me happy, the `predictedField` should be optional. If there is one specified, the model results should include predictions. If not, only anomalies.

To be honest, I don't see an issue with having a panel with an axis having a single symbol. I figured returning the same data structure regardless of the number of currency_pair fetched (only the number of elements within a dimension would change) would actually be a nice properties:

B) 

> I also noticed that
>    data=pdr.DataReader("EUR_USD", "oanda_historical_currency", "2014-03-19T09:00:00Z", "2014-03-20T09:00:00Z")
> returned
>  Date                  Close     High      Low     Open Volume Complete
>   2014-03-19 09:00:00  1.39142  1.39144  1.39141  1.39141      5     True
> while data["EUR_USD"]["Ask"] and data["EUR_USD"]["Bid"] raises a KeyError

OANDA's historical data (Close, Open, High, Low) are actually segregated into 3 candle prices: 
- Bid prices (B)
- Ask prices (A)
- Midpoint prices (M)

By default, the reader downloads only Midpoint prices. That's why querying Ask or Bid data raises a KeyError. The reader will by default download all 3 candle prices instead of only the midpoint prices.

I ended up using the following reification based approach, mostly because it's quite short (most of the below is doctest) and uses RDGA1 canonicalization that we already have in rdflib. Below I first convert each triple into a reified statement in a new graph, also converting Variables into BNodes. Then I use the RDGA1 canonicalization on that graph and afterwards re-extract the triples, transforming the renamed BNodes into renamed Variables. The result is a variable-name and order independent canonicalization of the BGP:

```python
def canonicalize_sparql_bgp(gp):
    """Returns a canonical basic graph pattern (BGP) with canonical var names.

    :param gp: a GraphPattern in form of a list of triples with Variables
    :return: A canonical GraphPattern with Variables renamed.

    >>> U = URIRef
    >>> V = Variable
    >>> gp1 = [
    ...     (V('blub'), V('bar'), U('blae')),
    ...     (V('foo'), V('bar'), U('bla')),
    ...     (V('foo'), U('poo'), U('blub')),
    ... ]
    >>> cgp = canonicalize(gp1)
    >>> v_blub = V('cb0')
    >>> v_bar = V(
    ...  'cb3d1b27f6269e23775a8da8d966dd669aa8262176ae6b938cccd653316791c42269')
    >>> v_foo = V(
    ...  'cb3b2718590899b3875a33cdc4aad060832711a614ee9c0ac83323f2e961bcc3f2db')
    >>> expected = [
    ...     (v_blub, v_bar, U('blae')),
    ...     (v_foo, v_bar, U('bla')),
    ...     (v_foo, U('poo'), U('blub'))
    ... ]
    >>> cgp == expected
    True

    To show that this is variable name and order independent we shuffle gp1 and
    rename its vars:
    >>> gp2 = [
    ...     (V('foonkyname'), V('baaar'), U('bla')),
    ...     (V('foonkyname'), U('poo'), U('blub')),
    ...     (V('funkyname'), V('baaar'), U('blae')),
    ... ]
    >>> cgp == canonicalize(gp2)
    True

    """
    assert isinstance(gp, Iterable)
    g = Graph()
    for t in gp:
        triple_bnode = BNode()
        s, p, o = [BNode(i) if isinstance(i, Variable) else i for i in t]
        g.add((triple_bnode, RDF['type'], RDF['Statement']))
        g.add((triple_bnode, RDF['subject'], s))
        g.add((triple_bnode, RDF['predicate'], p))
        g.add((triple_bnode, RDF['object'], o))
    cg = rdflib.compare.to_canonical_graph(g)
    cgp = []
    for triple_bnode in cg.subjects(RDF['type'], RDF['Statement']):
        t = [
            cg.value(triple_bnode, p)
            for p in [RDF['subject'], RDF['predicate'], RDF['object']]
        ]
        t = tuple([Variable(i) if isinstance(i, BNode) else i for i in t])
        cgp.append(t)
    return sorted(cgp)
```
