vsc-install provides shared setuptools functions and classes for python libraries developed by UGent's HPC group

Common pitfalls
=========
bdist_rpm will fail if your install_requires = 'setuptools' because it will fail to find a setuptools rpm.
```
export VSC_RPM_PYTHON=1
```
will make sure the `python-` prefix is added to the packages in install_requires for building RPM's so python-setuptools will be used.

Add tests
=========

Test are python modules in the `test` directory which have subclass of `TestCase`
and at least one method that has a name starting with `test_`

You are advised to use
```python
from vsc.install.testing import TestCase
```
(instead of basic `TestCase` from `unittest`).

And any `__main__` or `suite()` is not needed (anymore).

Initialise the test directory with

```bash
mkdir -p test
echo '' > test/__init__.py
echo 'from vsc.install.commontest import CommonTest' > test/00-import.py
```

When the tests are run, `test`, `lib` and `bin` (if relevant) are added to `sys.path`,
so no need to do so in the tets modules.

Run tests
=========

```bash
python setup.py test
```

Filter tests with `-F` (test module names) and `-f` (test method names)

See also

```bash
python setup.py test --help
```

In case following error occurs, it means there is a test module `XYZ` that cannot be imported.

```txt
File "setup.py", line 499, in loadTestsFromModule
    testsuites = ScanningLoader.loadTestsFromModule(self, module)
File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/setuptools/command/test.py", line 37, in loadTestsFromModule
File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/unittest/loader.py", line 100, in loadTestsFromName
    parent, obj = obj, getattr(obj, part)
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'XYZ'
```

You can try get the actual import error for fixing the issue with
```bash
python -c 'import sys;sys.path.insert(0, "test");import XYZ;'
```

Fix failing tests
=================

* Missing / incorrect `LICENSE`

 * Copy the appropirate license file under `known_licenses` in the project directory and name the file `LICENSE`

* Missing `README.md`

 * Create a `README.md` file with at least a `Description` section

* Fix license headers as described in https://github.com/hpcugent/vsc-install/blob/master/lib/vsc/install/headers.py

  ```
  cd <project dir with .git folder>
  REPO_BASE_DIR=$PWD python -m vsc.install.headers path/to/file script_or_not
  ```

  Fix them all at once using find

  ```
  find ./{lib,test} -type f -name '*.py' | REPO_BASE_DIR=$PWD xargs -I '{}' python -m vsc.install.headers '{}'
  find ./bin -type f -name '*.py' | REPO_BASE_DIR=$PWD xargs -I '{}' python -m vsc.install.headers '{}' 1
  ```

  Do not forget to check the diff
* Python scripts (i.e. with a python shebang and installed as scripts in setup) have to use `#!/usr/bin/env python` as shebang
* Remove any `build_rpms_settings.sh` leftovers
* The `TARGET` dict in `setup.py` should be minimal unless you really know what you are doing (i.e. if it is truly different from defaults)

 * Remove `name`, `scripts`, ...

* `Exception: vsc namespace packages do not allow non-shared namespace`

 * Add to the `__init__.py`

 ```python
 """
 Allow other packages to extend this namespace, zip safe setuptools style
 """
 import pkg_resources
 pkg_resources.declare_namespace(__name__)
 ```


bare-except
-----------
```python
try:
   # something
except:
```
This is bad, because this except will also catch sys.exit() or Keyboardinterupts, something you
typically do not want, if you catch these the program will be in a weird state and then continue on,
whilst the person who just pressed ctrl+c is wondering what is going on and why it is not stopping.

so at the very least make this
except Exception (which doesn't catch sys.exit and KeyboardInterupt)
and it would be appreciated if you could actually figure out what exceptions to expect and only catch those
and let your program crash if something you did not intend happens
because it helps developers catch weird errors on their side better.

if you do something like
```python
try:
    open(int(somestring)).write('important data')
except Exception:
    pass # if somestring is not an integer, we didn't need to write anyway, but otherwise we do
```
because you know sometimes this string does not contain an integer, so the int() call can fail
you should really only catch ValueError, because this will also fail when your disk is full, or you don't have permissions
or xxx other reasons, and the important data will not be written out and nobody will notice anything!



if not 'a' in somelist -> if 'a' not in somelist
-------------------------------------------------

this isn't that big of a deal, but if everyone is consistent it's less likely to introduce bugs when a not is added or removed where it didn't need to.
Also helps code review, not in reads better, like english.


arguments-differ
-----------------

this will give you errors if you override a function of a superclass but don't use the same amount of arguments,
using less will surely give you errors, so the linter catches this for you now

unused-argument
-----------------
if you have a function definition witch accepts an argument that is never used in the function body this will now give an error.
clean up your function definition, or fix the error where you actually do need to take this argument into account

unused-variable
----------------
defining a variable and then not using it anymore smells bad, why did you do that?
sometimes you do things like
```python
out, exit_code = run_command(something)
```
but you are not interested in the out, only in the exit code,
in this case, write
```python
_, exit_code = run_command(something)
```

using _ as a variable name lets pylint and other readers know you do not intend to use that output in the first place.


reimported
-------------
when you re import a name somewhere else,
usually this is just an import to much, or 2 imports with the same name, pay attention.
```python
import six
from django import six
```
=>
```python
import six
from django import six as django_six
```

redefinition of unused name
----------------------------
this usually also points to something you did not expect
```python
from vsc.accountpageclient import VscGroup
<snip>

class VscGroup(object):
    pass
```

=> do you need the import? use import as
did you mean to use the same name? ...

unpacking-in-except / redefine-in-handler
-----------------------------------------

Multiple exception have to be grouped in a tuple like

```python
    ...
except (ExceptionOne, ExceptionTwo) ...
    ...
```

(espcially when used like `except A, B:` which should be `except (A, B):`.

turning off these errors
-------------------------


If in any of these cases you think: yes, I really needed to do this,
I'm monkeypatching things, I'm adding extra functionality that does indeed have an extra(default) paramenter, etc, etc
you can let pylint know to ignore this error in this one specific block of code
by adding e.g. the comment `# pylint: disable=<name or numeric id of pylint code>`

```python
class Something(object):
    def dosomething(self, some, thing):
        # do something

class MyFancyThing(SomeThing):
    # pylint: disable=arguments-differ
    def dosomething(self, some, thing, fancy=None):
         # do something fancy
```

===========
vsc-install provides shared setuptools functions and classes for python libraries developed by UGent's HPC group

Common pitfalls
=========
bdist_rpm will fail if your install_requires = 'setuptools' because it will fail to find a setuptools rpm.
```
export VSC_RPM_PYTHON=1
```
will make sure the `python-` prefix is added to the packages in install_requires for building RPM's so python-setuptools will be used.

Add tests
=========

Test are python modules in the `test` directory which have subclass of `TestCase`
and at least one method that has a name starting with `test_`

You are advised to use
```python
from vsc.install.testing import TestCase
```
(instead of basic `TestCase` from `unittest`).

And any `__main__` or `suite()` is not needed (anymore).

Initialise the test directory with

```bash
mkdir -p test
echo '' > test/__init__.py
echo 'from vsc.install.commontest import CommonTest' > test/00-import.py
```

When the tests are run, `test`, `lib` and `bin` (if relevant) are added to `sys.path`,
so no need to do so in the tets modules.

Run tests
=========

```bash
python setup.py test
```

Filter tests with `-F` (test module names) and `-f` (test method names)

See also

```bash
python setup.py test --help
```

In case following error occurs, it means there is a test module `XYZ` that cannot be imported.

```txt
File "setup.py", line 499, in loadTestsFromModule
    testsuites = ScanningLoader.loadTestsFromModule(self, module)
File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/setuptools/command/test.py", line 37, in loadTestsFromModule
File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/unittest/loader.py", line 100, in loadTestsFromName
    parent, obj = obj, getattr(obj, part)
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'XYZ'
```

You can try get the actual import error for fixing the issue with
```bash
python -c 'import sys;sys.path.insert(0, "test");import XYZ;'
```

Fix failing tests
=================

* Missing / incorrect `LICENSE`

 * Copy the appropirate license file under `known_licenses` in the project directory and name the file `LICENSE`

* Missing `README.md`

 * Create a `README.md` file with at least a `Description` section

* Fix license headers as described in https://github.com/hpcugent/vsc-install/blob/master/lib/vsc/install/headers.py

  ```
  cd <project dir with .git folder>
  REPO_BASE_DIR=$PWD python -m vsc.install.headers path/to/file script_or_not
  ```

  Fix them all at once using find

  ```
  find ./{lib,test} -type f -name '*.py' | REPO_BASE_DIR=$PWD xargs -I '{}' python -m vsc.install.headers '{}'
  find ./bin -type f -name '*.py' | REPO_BASE_DIR=$PWD xargs -I '{}' python -m vsc.install.headers '{}' 1
  ```

  Do not forget to check the diff
* Python scripts (i.e. with a python shebang and installed as scripts in setup) have to use `#!/usr/bin/env python` as shebang
* Remove any `build_rpms_settings.sh` leftovers
* The `TARGET` dict in `setup.py` should be minimal unless you really know what you are doing (i.e. if it is truly different from defaults)

 * Remove `name`, `scripts`, ...

* `Exception: vsc namespace packages do not allow non-shared namespace`

 * Add to the `__init__.py`

 ```python
 """
 Allow other packages to extend this namespace, zip safe setuptools style
 """
 import pkg_resources
 pkg_resources.declare_namespace(__name__)
 ```


bare-except
-----------
```python
try:
   # something
except:
```
This is bad, because this except will also catch sys.exit() or Keyboardinterupts, something you
typically do not want, if you catch these the program will be in a weird state and then continue on,
whilst the person who just pressed ctrl+c is wondering what is going on and why it is not stopping.

so at the very least make this
except Exception (which doesn't catch sys.exit and KeyboardInterupt)
and it would be appreciated if you could actually figure out what exceptions to expect and only catch those
and let your program crash if something you did not intend happens
because it helps developers catch weird errors on their side better.

if you do something like
```python
try:
    open(int(somestring)).write('important data')
except Exception:
    pass # if somestring is not an integer, we didn't need to write anyway, but otherwise we do
```
because you know sometimes this string does not contain an integer, so the int() call can fail
you should really only catch ValueError, because this will also fail when your disk is full, or you don't have permissions
or xxx other reasons, and the important data will not be written out and nobody will notice anything!



if not 'a' in somelist -> if 'a' not in somelist
-------------------------------------------------

this isn't that big of a deal, but if everyone is consistent it's less likely to introduce bugs when a not is added or removed where it didn't need to.
Also helps code review, not in reads better, like english.


arguments-differ
-----------------

this will give you errors if you override a function of a superclass but don't use the same amount of arguments,
using less will surely give you errors, so the linter catches this for you now

unused-argument
-----------------
if you have a function definition witch accepts an argument that is never used in the function body this will now give an error.
clean up your function definition, or fix the error where you actually do need to take this argument into account

unused-variable
----------------
defining a variable and then not using it anymore smells bad, why did you do that?
sometimes you do things like
```python
out, exit_code = run_command(something)
```
but you are not interested in the out, only in the exit code,
in this case, write
```python
_, exit_code = run_command(something)
```

using _ as a variable name lets pylint and other readers know you do not intend to use that output in the first place.


reimported
-------------
when you re import a name somewhere else,
usually this is just an import to much, or 2 imports with the same name, pay attention.
```python
import six
from django import six
```
=>
```python
import six
from django import six as django_six
```

redefinition of unused name
----------------------------
this usually also points to something you did not expect
```python
from vsc.accountpageclient import VscGroup
<snip>

class VscGroup(object):
    pass
```

=> do you need the import? use import as
did you mean to use the same name? ...

unpacking-in-except / redefine-in-handler
-----------------------------------------

Multiple exception have to be grouped in a tuple like

```python
    ...
except (ExceptionOne, ExceptionTwo) ...
    ...
```

(espcially when used like `except A, B:` which should be `except (A, B):`.

turning off these errors
-------------------------


If in any of these cases you think: yes, I really needed to do this,
I'm monkeypatching things, I'm adding extra functionality that does indeed have an extra(default) paramenter, etc, etc
you can let pylint know to ignore this error in this one specific block of code
by adding e.g. the comment `# pylint: disable=<name or numeric id of pylint code>`

```python
class Something(object):
    def dosomething(self, some, thing):
        # do something

class MyFancyThing(SomeThing):
    # pylint: disable=arguments-differ
    def dosomething(self, some, thing, fancy=None):
         # do something fancy
```

Full list with all codes is available at http://pylint-messages.wikidot.com/all-codes

The problem here is that the pip errors are not granular enough to be useful the way I am using pip. If it told me it was a networking error specifically I could retry or failout with a useful message. But for most error cases I DO want to ignore the error since I am more interested in what packages got downloaded into my working directory since in general I combine a bunch of mutually optional steps that can fail and still have the overall deployment succeed. The only real error is a networking error.

One idea I had was to make a pull request to pip to separate out their error codes so that you can tell whether or not a particular error was networking related or not. But the way pip is now there is no useful information I can learn from the fact that pip failed parsing the output text which is possibly subject to change and not something I want to depend on. Simply wrapping pip with sys.exit is not acceptable since that breaks a lot of situations where a valid deployment package would have been built.

If you can give me a specific `requirements.txt` file that you think should be deployable I can take a look at it and see what can be done. So the problem appears to be that the deep learning AMI has a broken Boost installation. Two versions of the Boost libraries installed (1.53 in /usr/lib64, and 1.62 in /usr/lib), and when DyNet compiles it was finding the headers from one version 1.62, and the libraries from another 1.53. By removing the libraries from 1.53 I was able to fix the problem.

    mkdir ~/boost-quarantine
    sudo mv /usr/lib64/libboost* ~/boost-quarantine

Could you try this? For the short-term this work-around should work, and should not give you segfaults, although it may break other programs. In the long-term, I really want to get rid of the dependency on boost :)

This seems like the right time to bring up whether "WCS" is really a good description of what `gwcs` is for... WCS is certainly part of its purpose (specifically, it's the last co-ordinate frame in a chain of transformations), but more generally, `gwcs` deals with transformations between intermediate co-ordinate systems that are mostly _not_ World co-ordinates (unless you define World co-ordinates as anything other than raw pixel indices). I think this may be a source of ongoing confusion and misconception. I'm not sure whether this is a strong enough reason to change the package name, which I expect would be disruptive to STScI, or whether the distinction can simply be clarified in documentation, but it seems worth asking the question and settling it explicitly.

@scottcode sorry for the confusion. The docs have been updated since, but Vision hasn't been released yet.

`image` should not be a string. It should be an instance of `google.cloud.vision.image.Image`.

Also, the library doesn't yet support URLs for image sources.

To fix you could download the image with [`requests`](http://requests.readthedocs.io/en/master) and then pass the content to Vision.

```python
image = client.image(content=image_content)
results = client.annotation(image, [feature])
```

Or you can do single annotations on an image like so.
```python
image = client.image(content=image_content)
results = image.detect_labels()
```

Some significant changes are coming in the next release, so you may want to pin the version of Vision you're using.

> I'm not quite sure what the proposed sub-workunits would be at the moment :) Is this just for the lazy stdout/stderr case? Or because output files in general should only be lazily fetched to avoid unnecessary network calls? Or something else?

With no sub-workunits, you would say that "all process output created during this synchronous invocation of the engine is flattened into a single workunit" (which would be fine for now, because each engine invoke will result in one compiler invoke).

Once an invoke of the engine results in _multiple_ processes being invoked (ie, once we port from multiple synchronous invokes: one per compile to one synchronous invoke for many compiles), you would want sub-workunits of some kind.

Ah, yes, this is confusing. The idea was to warn if you are relying on --default-backend-packages (i.e., using any backends from it that aren't also provided by --backend-packages).  This gave us an opportunity to detect any overwriting of --backend-packages when the intention should be to add to them.

The remedy for the warning in this case, though, is to remove the unwanted backends from --default-backend-packages.   The warning message should probably have explained that, sorry.

But, a note re the necessary length of the deprecation cycle:  The thing being deprecated isn't --default-backend-packages per se, it's the availability of hard-coded default backends. And that's been present forever. The purpose of this deprecation was to prevent repos that override --backend-packages from suddenly losing all the default backends with no warning, and having no way of figuring out wtf.

So a more cautious alternative is to see the deprecation through, but change the warning message to explain that once you're happy with the state of your --backend-packages you can set --default-backend-packages to an empty list to remove the warnings.

I think we're in agreement. I think the proper behavior is that if you send a TERM to one of the children:
- diamond as a whole won't die
- diamond won't start misbehaving (busy loops, exceptions printed out etc.)

That's the case for the most part here. The `while True` loop actually respawns processes that should be running -- with the exception of SyncManager of course.

As such, I think Diamond should be coded so that the signal handler currently found in the "main" binary only runs on the main process. The SyncManager process should probably have SIG_IGN for TERM/INT, and the rest of the children should probably have a signal handler to allow them to just die gracefully themselves without the code for enumerating over multiprocessing processes at all like they have now.

For anyone having the same problem try executing this

~~~
docker ps -a | grep "Exited" | grep -v "hours" | grep -v deis | cut -f1 -d" " | xargs docker rm
docker images -q | xargs -l10 docker rmi
~~~

This will remove all containers that are exited (and are not beloinging to deis), more than 2 days ago + all the images that can be safely removed.

Note the second command is slightly different from the docs, as the -a is not needed (I believe), and will make the command slower to execute (plus display more scary errors) than necessary.

Note this will still not remove all old images, it still keeps the following:
A) previous versions from images from deis components (as those containers are not cleaned up)
B) When your registry has moved, and your node has the same tagged image from 2 different repositories it wont remove them either. This also needs a -f (force) flag, but is not only limited to that error so you can not include it blindly. In theory you can get the errors from the second command, filter out this specific message and rerun with the -f flag. This case is not too common so I did not include it. @carver looking at the [docs for python3](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#metaclass-example), it looks like metaclasses *can* maintain field ordering.

```python
import collections

class OrderedClass(type):

    @classmethod
    def __prepare__(metacls, name, bases, **kwds):
        return collections.OrderedDict()

    def __new__(cls, name, bases, namespace, **kwds):
        result = type.__new__(cls, name, bases, dict(namespace))
        result.members = tuple(namespace.keys())
        result.base_members = [tuple(base.members) for base in bases if hasattr(base, 'members')]
        return result

class A(metaclass=OrderedClass):
    def one(self): pass
    def two(self): pass
    def three(self): pass
    def four(self): pass


class B(A):
    def five(self): pass


>>> A.members
('__module__', '__qualname__', 'one', 'two', 'three', 'four')
>>> A.base_members
[]
>>> B.members
('__module__', '__qualname__', 'five')
>>> B.base_members
[('__module__', '__qualname__', 'one', 'two', 'three', 'four')]
```

With that information, we would be able to maintain field ordering within a class, as well as treating *new* fields in subclasses as they should be *appended* by default with `cls.__mro__` for ordering of base classes.  That makes our default behavior:

- append to end if new field
- replace current field if override

That gets us *most* of the way there.  I think the only thing we need to allow full control over field ordering is a way to dictate *where* in the previous class's fields a new field should go assuming they don't want to use the default ordering.  Two options come to mind for dealing with this and we might want to do both (or some other better idea).

#### Support `before` and `after` keyword arguments for the `Field` class.

The `Field` class could take one of the keyword arguments `before` or `after` which would specify a the position that the new field should be placed in the field order.

```python3
class A:
    one = Field()
    two = Field()
    four = Field()

class B(A):
    three = Field(after='two')
    # three = Field(before='four')

B.fields = ('one', 'two', 'three', 'four')
```

#### Support meta params of some sort.

Borrowed **heavily** from django, we could support something like the following for explicitely declaring field ordering.

```python
class A:
    one = Field()
    two = Field()
    four = Field()

class B(A):
    three = Field()

    class Meta:
        field_order = ('one', 'two', 'three', 'four')

B.fields = ('one', 'two', 'three', 'four')
```

We've run into this with nbconvert as well. I setup [a repo](https://github.com/minrk/debug-conda-appveyor) to do some testing:

On AppVeyor, installing nbconvert from conda-forge has started failing with:

```
ERROR conda.core.link:_execute_actions(337): An error occurred while installing package 'conda-forge::nbconvert-5.3.1-py_1'.
CondaError: Cannot link a source that does not exist. C:\Miniconda-x64\Scripts\conda.exe
Attempting to roll back.
CondaError: Cannot link a source that does not exist. C:\Miniconda-x64\Scripts\conda.exe
```

Possibly relevant bits of info:

- `conda clean --all` does not help
- Failure only occurs on [Windows + Python 2](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/minrk/debug-conda-appveyor/build/1.0.9)
- It only occurs when conda is upgraded or downgraded during the same install. Disabling auto_update_conda [fixes the issue](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/minrk/debug-conda-appveyor/build/1.0.10)
- Appears to be a regression between conda [4.3.8](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/minrk/debug-conda-appveyor/build/1.0.21) and [4.3.9](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/minrk/debug-conda-appveyor/build/1.0.22)
- nbconvert has `noarch: python` and an entry-point, as does tqdm
- installing last nbconvert release prior to noarch [has no issue](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/minrk/debug-conda-appveyor/build/1.0.11)
- installing other, lighter packages with noarch + entrypoint (geojsonio) [doesn't reproduce the issue](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/minrk/debug-conda-appveyor/build/1.0.6) so there might be something else special about nbconvert+tqdm, or it could just be the ordering of packages causing the correct sequence of events to occur that `conda.exe` is removed while it's attempted to be linked.

Ah! conda was copying that file and setting the permissions as such.  `/miniconda/*` is (again) a read-only tree and owned by root. So, `chmod` the files there as 'u+x', which would fix the problem as well..

```
sudo chown -R root:root /miniconda/pkgs  # optional
sudo chmod -R ug+w /miniconda/pkgs
```
Which fixed the problem!

Looking specifically at https://github.com/gogetdata/ggd-recipes/tree/master/recipes/Canis_familiaris/canFam3/canfam3-dbsnp for example, it looks to me like what you actually *want* is post-link rather than pre-link.  The meta.yaml lists `gsort` as a run requirement, and I see `gsort` being used in the script, but that's only guaranteed to be available if the script is post-link and not pre-link.

So in short: no need for a `-p` or `--prefix` keyword. Just supply the full path to the environment.

It raises a question: what should the behavior be with _relative_ paths? The problem, of course, is that `activate `_name_, when _name_ is both an environment name and the name of a subdirectory, will be ambiguous. And yet, I think relative paths would be useful.

I'm honestly not sure what the current behavior is, but I would suggest the following: if the argument to `activate` starts _or ends_ with a slash, it should indicate a path-based environment selection.

Ah. The `--use-local` flag is equivalent to `-c local`.  There is literally a special channel named `local`, which is actually the conda-build channel, and therefore sort of a confusing name.  Unfortunately, it's historic and difficult to change, so we're stuck with it.

I didn't even know `--show-channel-urls` was a valid flag for the `install` command.  The `--force` we're trying to get away from; it means too many different things in too many different contexts.

For what you're trying to do, I'd recommend just

    conda install --dry-run -c file:///tmp sparkmagic

or

    conda install --dry-run file:///tmp::sparkmagic

The reason for the feature is that I have an embedded system project that uses Python for the UI, and I am using a ConfigObj as the central configuration management object.  When a configuration value changes, I want to do two things:

1) See if the option is one that should be persisted between reboots.  If so, save the updated configuration to a file.
2) Emit a message to the UI event loop to signal that a configuration value has changed and that the UI needs to update the appropriate sections.

I agree on passing in the ConfigObj rather than just the name.  That would be less ambiguous, and doesn't require assumptions as to the section or key naming conventions that the developer is using.

Agreed also on point #2.  I can see benefits to both approaches (only setting the on_change callback on instantiation or automatically propagate to children).  The easiest would be to only set it on initialization.  Automatic propagation would be more powerful, but I can envision cases where this could cause confusion.  Setting it on subsections would also be powerful, but would possibly be confusing for most use cases (when to propagate, when not to, should it override an already set callback, etc.).  To take it to an extreme, I can see how one would want to set different on_change callbacks on different sections to have appropriate actions take place  when a specific value changes, such as emitting a Qt signal, post to a queue, or some other event.  I handle that decision tree in my code by declaring a single on_change callback in the top-level ConfigObj, use unique names for all ConfigObj sections and keys, and then look at the section and key that was changed to determine what to do.  However, my way is my way and their way is their way, and that's OK.  We just need to handle it in the least confusing way.

One point regarding the series of on_change callback calls that you show in your first example code block is that I personally am only changing individual values after the ConfigObj is initially loaded, so the cascading calls haven't really been an issue.   But I can see how it may cause some confusion.  I'm going to think about this a little bit to see if there is a more elegant solution.

I would advocate that I/we look at the following if you are willing to consider accepting a patch:

1) Only allow setting an on_change callback on initialization of ConfigObj for now.
2) When the on_change callback is called, the first parameter should be the ConfigObj instance, not the section name.

`reprepro` is to blame, as it doesn't seem to support the desired behavior (preserving older versions in `Packages`.  I switched to using `aptly` for the Curator 4 repository, but have not done so for Curator 5 (and may never do so for that branch).  It's a huge risk taking down one published repo to put up another, and hope it all goes smoothly, with all paths properly preserved.  This will probably be addressed in a more permanent fashion for Curator 6.

If you really need to pull down prior versions, you should be able to specify `MAJOR`.`MINOR`.`PATCH` for the URL:

```
https://packages.elastic.co/curator/5/debian/pool/main/e/elasticsearch-curator/elasticsearch-curator_MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH_amd64.deb
```

You should almost _never_ have to do this, though.  I strive to ensure reverse-compatibility and not break functionality in minor point releases.  Anything 5.1 will still do everything 5.0 will do, but with fewer bugs, and some new functionality.  You should be able to always feel comfortable upgrading.

[[ Problem]]
The wheel package registered in the git repository fails in the installation on the Azure environment. 

[[ Primary cause ]]
on Python 3.4 of Azure Web Apps, version of pip is 1.5.6 .
pip (1.5.6) is not able to recognize '--find-links' option which was written in the top of requirements.txt file.

[[ Workarround ]]
Upgrade pip on Azure Web Apps, using 'deploy.cmd' file.

[[ Detail ]]
At first, please refer to following URL. 
  https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/app-service-web/web-sites-python-configure

I got default '.deployment' file and 'deploy.cmd' file from Azure Web Apps Kudu Service.
** How to get these files **
1. Login to azure dashboard
2. Select Web Apps instance
3. Select [Advanced Tools] and view new page which title is 'Kudu Services'
4. Select [Tools] at menu bar, and choose [Download deployment script]
   --> Save 'deploymentscript.zip' file into local.
5. Unzip deploymentscript.zip

and, please edit please edit the vicinity of the following lines in 'deploy.cmd' .
  (before)
    echo Pip install requirements.
    env\scripts\pip install -r requirements.txt
  (after)
    echo Upgrade pip package.
    env\scripts\python -m pip install pip --upgrade
    IF !ERRORLEVEL! NEQ 0 goto error
    echo Pip install requirements.
    env\scripts\pip install -r requirements.txt

  (* I also upgraded setuptools )

finally, please copy '.deployment' and 'deploy.cmd' file onto top folder of GIT project,
then commit/push it.
 
By this workarrownd, I successfully installed wheel package into Azure Web Apps.
It would be greatly appreciated if this method is useful for you.

> To me, if the same error code can be associated with significantly different error messages, then something is wrong with the design of the source code. An error code should always mean the same thing. For example, "file not found" might be the message for an error code, and the specific file might vary, but I wouldn't want the same error code to also mean "file permission error". 

I am not sure that it is good error codes design.  "file not found" can be raised in totally different cases with different recovery procedure:

1. Example one. File name can be provided by application and it is precondition checking that can be recovered.
2. Example two. It can be raised by storage backend and it is invariant checking. Most probable in this case only application panic is possible.

Same with json. We need different error codes for a case when user passed invalid json and for a case when we can't parse value stored in the wallet.

So in error codes design we are more thinking about recovery procedure than propagation of low-level error by specific error code.

I like this, but:

Simple types will still need to receive `env` if we're going to move `context` into it.

I agree with @lkraider about `context` being a user-generated data bag. The intent was that a user could pass in information telling types how to export (or import) data within the context of the current situation. The canonical example is the `MultiLingualString`, where it can export to many different languages but won't know which one at instantiation time. The same could be true for imports. Say you don't know what locale you'll be parsing data from at model creation time, but don't want a hypothetical `StrictlyLocalizedFloatType` to accept "123,45" in a `en_US` context because that's not a valid representation in America, and "123.45" isn't valid in `de_AT`. We should support `context` in the import loop symmetrically to how it's supported in the export loop.

Since this is a (mildly) breaking change, we'd need to bump the minor version at least.

As of 1c69ae8f28e70e923ce5413ac247e405565634b8, the implicit default value is `Undefined`.

What this means is that `None` is now also a legal default, and it's possible to make a distinction between NULL and *nothing*. The standard behavior remains such that all fields are initialized to `None` upon instance creation, but setting `init_values=False` will prevent this. Then, only those fields that explicitly declare `default=None` will be initialized to `None`.

Here's a first attempt at drafting how we might summarize it in our documentation (please improve this, or counter with something else!):

For scalar-output functions `f`, `grad(f)` computes the gradient you expect. For vector-output functions `f`, if we have that `f(x)[i] == f(x[i])`, as in the "vectorized" or "map" case where scalar-input numpy functions like `np.tanh` are called on an ndarray input, then we have `grad(f)(x)[i] == grad(f)(x[i])`. (For more general vector-output functions, `grad(f)` evaluates the Jacobian-adjoint operator applied to an all-ones vector, so that `grad(f)(x) == np.tensordot(jacobian(f)(x).H, np.ones_like(f(x)), f(x).ndim)`.)

Let me see if I can summarize here:

# Requirements
* Addresses must have version numbers (let's start with `0`)
* Addresses must have checksums (e.g. first 4 bytes of double-sha256 of address)
* Addresses should not have any "ambiguous" character sets.  For example, `0` and `O` cannot both be characters in the encoding, and nor can `1` and `l`.
* Addresses must not "look like" any other cryptocurrency address.
* Addresses cannot have punctuation
* The encoding scheme must be efficient at handling things like zero-padding.

## Prefix Annotations
To make it unambiguous, I highly recommend a short prefix be appended to addresses.  This seems to have worked well for Ethereum, for example---a long hex string starting with `0x` is assumed to be an Ethereum address.

The choice of prefix must not conflict with the encoding alphabet letters.

# Examples
Let `395f3643cea07ec4eec73b4d9a973dcce56b9bf1` be the account's hexadecimal representation.

Let the version be `00`.

Then, the checksum is `double_sha256(version + '395f3643cea07ec4eec73b4d9a973dcce56b9bf1')[:8] = '435e3bc5'`.

So, we'll really be encoding `00395f3643cea07ec4eec73b4d9a973dcce56b9bf1435e3bc5` as our example.

## Hex (Base 16)
* Alphabet: `0123456789abcdef`
* Example: `00395f3643cea07ec4eec73b4d9a973dcce56b9bf1435e3bc5`

## Base32 
* Alphabet: `ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ234567`
* Example: `A4V6NSDZ2QH5RHOY45U3GUXHXGOK2436FBV4O6F`

## Z-Base-32
* Alphabet: `ybndrfg8ejkmcpqxot1uwisza345h769`
* Example: `yhi6p1d34o87t8qah7w5gwz8zgqk4h56fbihq6f`

## Cockford Base-32
* Alphabet: `0123456789ABCDEFGHJKMNPQRSTVWXYZ`
* Example: `0WNYDJ3STG7XH7ERWXMV6MQ7Q6EATWVY51NWEY5`

## Base58 (Bitcoin)
* Alphabet: `123456789ABCDEFGHJKLMNPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijkmnopqrstuvwxyz`
* Example: `16EMaNw3pkn3v6f2BgnSSs53zAKH4Q8YJg`

## Base64 (no padding)
* Alphabet: `ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789+/`
* Example: `AOV82Q86gfsTuxztNmpc9zOVrm/FDXjvF`

Your example can be implemented more cleanly by registering a ``splinter_browser_load_condition`` fixture. However for single page apps it's tricky to come up with conditions that always work. Luckily for the most part that's not an issue as selenium's implicit wait does the job, but for those conditions that are evaluated python side there's no option but to loop waiting for the condition.

I don't think excessive waiting is likely to be a problem here. Let's take splinter's ``is_text_present`` method as an example:
```python
    def is_text_present(self, text, wait_time=None):
        wait_time = wait_time or self.wait_time
        end_time = time.time() + wait_time

        while time.time() < end_time:
            try:
                self.driver.find_element_by_tag_name('body').text.index(text)
                return True
            except ValueError:
                pass
            except NoSuchElementException:
                # This exception will be thrown if the body tag isn't present
                # This has occasionally been observed. Assume that the
                # page isn't fully loaded yet
                pass
        return False
```

Excessive waiting might be a problem if the selenium implicit wait time were less than the wait_time, as multiple implicit waits could be triggered. But by setting them to the same value that should not be a concern. If we consider a wait time and implicit wait time of 5s under the following possibilities:

*  The page is not loaded at all.
  - On the first iteration ``find_element_by_tag_name('body')`` will pause for 5s before raising.
  - The while condition while then be false as we are past the end time so we don't loop around again.

* The page has loaded but the text is never present.
  - On the first iteration ``find_element_by_tag_name('body')`` returns the element and but the text is not found.
  - Each subsequent iteration we expect that ``find_element_by_tag_name('body')`` will return quickly (as selenium will not need to implicitly wait).
  - As loops are fairly fast it will return quite soon after the wait_time has elapsed.

I have experienced same issue with v1.5. It was due to ```get_ancestors()``` call while ```get_absolute_url()``` has got called. So I have done a hack in my overriden Category class as following -

```python
    def _get_ancestors_cache_key(self):
        paths = [
            self.path[0:pos]
            for pos in range(0, len(self.path), self.steplen)[1:]
        ]
        key = str(paths)
        return key.replace(',', '').replace('\'', '').replace('[','N').replace(']','N').replace(' ', '')

    def get_ancestors(self):
        key = self._get_ancestors_cache_key()
        cached_val = cache.get(key)
        if cached_val is not None:
            # print("Found cache for %s", str(paths))
            return cached_val

        result = super(AbstractCategory, self).get_ancestors()
        cache.set(key, result)

        return result
```

I've created a few diagrams to aid in discussion.

One issue that I've found is that the placement of attributes can be very confusing. If you're trying to write a query to find all products that have a given attribute value, you have to check on the child as well as on the parent. For instance, if you have one parent, one child, and 3 attribute values, there are actually 8 different ways that they can be configured. Here's 3 of them:

![attribute_issue_1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2071543/10075820/8cd2df70-629d-11e5-8b5d-b7b9f2461dda.png)

Obviously, if you have multiple children, it gets even more complicated.

Because attribute values can be placed on the parent, what does it "mean" when you create a child? Should that child silently "inherit" the attribute values of the parent? Here's a concrete example. Imagine you have a T-shirt that comes in a large, so you create a parent-child relationship with the "Large" attribute value on the parent. Later, you add a medium size as a child, but you leave the "Large" attribute value on the parent. Is that new child implicitly a large size? Does it inherit the attribute value on the parent? Even more confusingly, what if you add a a "Medium" attribute value to the new child? Now you have some potentially conflicting attribute values on both the parent and child.

![attribute_issue_2 1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2071543/10076000/97fc935e-629e-11e5-8130-42de4703e33a.png)

Lastly, a parent-child relationship with a single child is functionally identical to a standalone product. So, you might create a standalone product, only later to realize that you needed to add another variant. So you have to remove the stockrecord, add a child for the old stockrecord, add a new instance of the old stockrecord to the first child, and then you can actually add a new child. This is confusing and error prone.

![attribute_issue_3](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2071543/10076096/283f0960-629f-11e5-8438-1171fb017b54.png)

One solution is to remove the concept of a standalone product, and enforce that every product follows the parent-child product. In addition, you move the attribute value relationship to be only on the child, it fixes pretty much all of the attribute value issues.

So that's what I did. Here's my branch: https://github.com/john-parton/django-oscar/tree/issue/675/product_to_parent

This is more than a simple proof of concept. _Every test passes._ (Some had to be tweaked or rewritten, but they're all there.)

On the frontend, it's functionally identical, if not slightly better. For instance, in the old way, if a product is standalone, you can have an "add to cart" button directly under the product. (Neat, that's one fewer step in the funnel.) However, if you had a parent-child relationship with a single child, you don't get that button, even though it's functionally identical to a standalone product. In my new way, you automatically get that button if there's only one child.

The `Product` model has been broken out into two models `Product` and `ChildProduct`. (I would slightly prefer a name like "ProductVariant", but the term "child" is used throughout the code base, so I made it that for consistency.) https://github.com/john-parton/django-oscar/blob/issue/675/product_to_parent/src/oscar/apps/catalogue/abstract_models.py#L241-L593

If you look at the `clean` methods for the models, you should immediately notice something versus the old way: there really isn't anything to them.

There are caveats, of course (there are always caveats):

* I haven't provided any migrations.
* If a overrode the product app, things will probably break.
* If a user has a custom model with a relation to `Product`, it'll probably break.
* Minimal changes were necessary to the templates, but again, there might be some edge cases.

> In fact pandas must have some rules as to how they format timedeltas; see http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/timedeltas.html#reductions where they put the minus sign in front of the largest number

Note that it seems be do not much different than stdlib timedelta:

```
In [55]: y2 = pd.Series(pd.to_timedelta(['-1 days +00:00:05', 'nat', '-1 days +00:00:05', '1 days']))

In [56]: y2
Out[56]: 
0   -1 days +00:00:05
1                 NaT
2   -1 days +00:00:05
3     1 days 00:00:00
dtype: timedelta64[ns]

In [64]: [print(i) for i in y2.dt.to_pytimedelta()]
-1 day, 0:00:05
NaT
-1 day, 0:00:05
1 day, 0:00:00
```

so main difference is that we put a `+` sign to clarify the confusing repr a bit (and it is still not the most easy one, you could also have "-23:59:55" instead of "-1 days +00:00:05")

Pandas has an `.isoformat()` method on the Timedelta class (so deviating from stdlib timedelta), recently implemented in https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/15136 (although no way to read this format back in ...)

> The pandas case is interesting, but I suspect that they solved the much easier problem of creating a reasonable string format for a timedelta, not the general case of a formatting mini-spec

Yes, I think that is correct. We have a `to_timedelta` function to convert arbitrary objects to timedelta, but (opposed to `to_datetime`) it currently does not support a `format` arg. See http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/timedeltas.html#to-timedelta 
So we only parse 'standard' interpretable strings, but that certainly already leads to some corner cases as well (eg https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/9570 on '0:00:00' vs '00:00:00').

I looked at that issue. I think I'm concerned about something different.

My concern is that there's no pluggable step in deserialization that happens before the deserialization of individual fields. The current pipeline for deserialization looks like:

1. Deserialize fields into dictionary
2. Run preprocessors
3. Run validators
4. Make object

By contrast, the pipeline for serialization looks like:

1. Serialize fields into dictionary
2. Run postprocessor/data handlers

I believe @davidism is talking about adding something before (1) above in serialization. My concern is different - it's that there's nothing before (1) in deserialization, that can reverse the effect of a data handler.

For example, I can't reverse the effects of the data handler in the example with a preprocessor: http://marshmallow.readthedocs.org/en/latest/extending.html#transforming-data

Additionally, because preprocessing is done on `fields.Unmarshaller` instead of on a method on the `Schema` object like `Schema._postprocess`, I can't even conveniently override any of the relevant methods, like I do to work around #177.

Again, sorry for the delay. I'm going to hold off on making changes to the current Query fields. There are multiple ways to write them, and I'm still not sure they belong in marshmallow core. For the time being, I'll add a warning in the docs and leave more optimized implementations up to external packages.

Thanks for taking a look @sloria !  I realize this is quite the PR, but the perf gains are pretty massive.  It allows users to have the great API marshmallow provides with speeds close to what they would be if they hand-rolled their validation.

There's some perf improvements here that are unrelated to this change, but only yielded meaningful improvements once the parsing code was jit'd (e.g. 100µs wouldn't be worth it with the current implementation, but when jitted it is).  We could potentially pull those into a separate PR.  It seems safest to have some of those behind a flag

If we were to take the separate API approach, the API I'd want is something like this:

```python
from marshmallow_jit import jit

class Foo(Schema):
   ....

foo_schema = jit(Foo())
```

In this world, we'd need 2 hooks.  One for invoking the jitted method instead of `Marshaller` and falling back to `self._marshal` if needed (https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/pull/573/files#diff-035365c88325ed9779fcb020f7bcb35aR660) and one for recompiling the generated methods on `update_fields` - https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/pull/573/files#diff-035365c88325ed9779fcb020f7bcb35aR933

In the future there may need to be other hooks, next would likely be deserialize and perhaps a fast-path for schemas without processors.

1) (although I didn't know that you could do that) it is very non-intuitive. What is intuitive is having a field type for each of your container types which actually do the job correctly.
2) This works only for creating fields that are collections of other objects (which are described with another Schema class derivatives). If you want to create a list of simple, non-object types (e.g. IPAddress field which actually is just a customized string), you can not (or at least, should not) use fields.Nested.

I'm wondering when the complexity of a field grows enough that it needs to be split into two fields. Currently, a type like `int` has one field and we try to support all common serialization and deserialization formats through field options. In this case the complexity is pretty low so adding an extra argument or two is probably acceptable, but it might be useful to consider all the use cases:

 - `Number | String -> Integer` (default behavior)
 - `String | Number -> Integer` (`as_string` prefers string serialization)
 - `Number -> Integer` (strict behavior)
 - `String -> Integer` (string only format is currently not supported)

One way to model this is to decompose the base cases into strict fields (like `IntegerString` and `IntegerNumber`) that specialize in strict conversion. That would allow us to compose them into a more complex field with opinionated defaults (like `Integer`) without needing additional options to reduce it to one of the strict fields.

The solution here depends on the way how we want multiple contracts to be treated.

Currently, Mythril loads all contracts and maps them to different addresses (0x0000(...), 0x1111(...)). It then execute the *first* contract as the entrypoint (see also #58). The idea was to allow analysis of multiple interdependent contracts.

It seems that this behavior is confusing, as users expect the contracts to be analyzed on-by-one. 

Would it make sense to change this to be in line with expectations, and allow multi-contract analysis only for on-chain targets?

@cwhanse @wholmgren So there is some question as to if the computation of model residuals that I use to gauge the quality of solutions is sufficiently well computed numerically (as opposed to being mathematically sound, which seems sufficiently obvious to me). Perhaps we should simplify the logic for this PR and only check if the series resistance is zero (respectively, parallel resistance is infinity) and compute using the explicit solution in only those cases. We could leave the very near ideal cases where the LambertW computation seems inaccurate to future work. This would mean that this PR should be guaranteed to not make any computations worse.

This said, I would claim that I have seen no better evidence that the LambertW solution is sufficiently well computed numerically (as opposed to being mathematically sound, which is well documented in the literature), and maybe we should write a paper that, for many cases, numerically checks the residuals of the implicit model equation for both solutions using "provable" interval analysis, and maybe even some numerical analysis proofs that could put all these computations on a firmer standing.

     (declare-rel atom/cidr/mask (cidr bits/32 bits/32 bits/32))
     (rule (forall ((x bits/32) (y bits/32) (z bits/32)) (=> (and (atom/cidr x y) (= z (bvand x y))) (atom/cidr/mask x y z))))

     ;(rule (forall ((CidrBase2 bits/32) (CidrMask2 bits/32) (CidrBase1 bits/32) (Cidr2 cidr) (Cidr1 cidr) (CidrMask1 bits/32))
     ;  (=> (and (= (bvand CidrBase2 CidrMask2) (bvand CidrBase1 CidrMask2))
     ;           (atom/cidr Cidr2 CidrBase2 CidrMask2)
     ;           (atom/cidr Cidr1 CidrBase1 CidrMask1))
     ;      (cidrs-subset Cidr1 Cidr2))))

    (rule (forall ((CidrBase2 bits/32) (CidrMask2 bits/32) (CidrBase1 bits/32) (m bits/32) (Cidr2 cidr) (Cidr1 cidr) (CidrMask1 bits/32))
       (=> (and (atom/cidr/mask Cidr2 CidrBase2 CidrMask2 m)

@delcypher: The Visual Studio backend of cmake produces a solution and project files that aren't usable. They overwhelm VS with a lot of tiny sub-projects. So much so, that it literally takes minutes before it can _start_ a build. Further, all those little sub-projects have their own compilation settings, so if we need to change a compiler option, we have to either click millions of times, or hack the project files outside of VS. 

The philosophy in VS is a bit different; usually there would be one sub-project for each product, i.e. libz3.dll, z3.exe, c_example, etc, instead of projects that produce static libraries and sub-modules can be within folders or project filters (see currently used .vcxproj files). VS does it's own dependency analysis and incremental compilation features. How much effort is it to teach cmake to do that?

For datasets I think this can be "hacked" right now because you can **1)** enforce that a dataset gets chunked and can be expanded by wrapping it with H5DataIO and setting the maxshape (and chunking) **2)** you have access to the h5py.Dataset after read. E.g., the following seems to work, although I think this might be more of a hack than an intended function:

```
# Create some example data
from datetime import datetime
from pynwb import NWBFile

start_time = datetime(2017, 4, 3, 11, 0, 0)
create_date = datetime(2017, 4, 15, 12, 0, 0)

nwbfile = NWBFile('PyNWB tutorial', 'demonstrate NWBFile basics', 'NWB123', start_time,
                  file_create_date=create_date)

from pynwb import TimeSeries
from pynwb.form.backends.hdf5.h5_utils import H5DataIO

data = H5DataIO(data=list(range(100, 200, 10)), maxshape=(None,))  # Make sure the HDF5 dataset can be exbanded
timestamps =  H5DataIO(data=list(range(10)), maxshape=(None,))     # Make sure the HDF5 dataset can be exbanded
test_ts = TimeSeries('test_timeseries', 'PyNWB tutorial', data, 'SIunit', timestamps=timestamps)
nwbfile.add_acquisition(test_ts)

# Write the data to file
from pynwb import NWBHDF5IO
io = NWBHDF5IO('basic_example.nwb', 'w')
io.write(nwbfile)
io.close()

# Read teh data back in
io = NWBHDF5IO('basic_example.nwb')
nwbfile = io.read()
ts = nwbfile.get_acquisition('test_timeseries')
print("Current data")
print("Data:" , ts.data[:])
print("Timestamps", ts.timestamps[:])

# Expand the HDF5 datasets
ts.data.resize((20,))
ts.timestamps.resize((20,))
ts.data[10:] = list(range(10))
ts.timestamps[10:] = list(range(10,20,1))
# Close and reopen the file to confirm the data was written
io.close()
del(nwbfile)

# Read the data back in to confirm the expansion worked
io = NWBHDF5IO('basic_example.nwb')
nwbfile = io.read()
ts = nwbfile.get_acquisition('test_timeseries')
print("Data:" , ts.data[:])
print("Timestamps", ts.timestamps[:])
```

Note that the "chain of dependencies" also means that we cannot actually perform "true" unit tests on any objects that have other objects as required attributes, since they can't be created until the "upstream" object is created, and it can't be created until it's upstream object is created, etc, etc.

For example, the current unit test for DfOverF creates the following just to test the DfOverF class

- 1 `RoiResponseSeries`
- 1 `ImageSegmentation`
- 1 `PlaneSegmentation`
- 2 `ROI`s 
- 1 `ImagingPlane`
- 1 `ImageSeries`
- 1 `OpticalChannel`

An error in any of these classes will cause the "unit test" for DfOverF to fail.

This is a side effect of pushing schema validation (via docval) to object creation.

I think discovery in FxA is based on BrowserID? So:
* the user gives their email address me@example.com to a Kinto client
* the Kinto client retrieves [https://example.com/.well-known/browserid](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/Persona/.well-known-browserid)
* if this resource does not exist for example.com, it falls back to BrowserID's [secondary identity provider](http://lloyd.io/how-browserid-works/), from there to Mozilla's default FxA instance, and then to Mozilla's default Kinto instance.

So I think what a user has to do to self-host and be entirely free from any hard-coded Mozilla URLs, is to become their own primary identity provider for BrowserID, plus FxA, plus Kinto?

There is still a difference between remoteStorage and Kinto in that remoteStorage redirects the user to https://remotestorage.io/get/ which lists multiple options for getting an account on a storage provider somewhere, and I think Kinto currently would default silently to Mozilla's instance. So you could insert a page in the UI saying 'You have no Kinto server linked to me@example.com yet, do you want to link Mozilla's server?', and then that would be a point where multiple options could be offered.

Of course, the user experience is easier when defaulting to Mozilla-hosted is silent. But I think the difference boils down only to whether this is silent or not; in itself, BrowserId already implements decentralized discovery.

Hi, what you are reporting makes sense, since the rs::context is managed like a singleton but not really. Originally, librealsense included a limitation allowing only a single instance of context at any given time. Later down the line, my team has somewhat relaxed this restriction based on feedback from ROS community, allowing context to be ref-counted. We intentionally avoided something like Mayers singleton, in order to provide deterministic destruction (once last instance of context is destroyed, we would like all RealSense resources to be deallocated). This might have caused issues when linking statically. This is a purely software issue and hence can be investigated and fixed.

However, I don't think it is possible to use dynamic config value as a `CharField` attribute. `max_length` is enforced by the database server, which is set by Django upon table creation. You will need to makemigrations/migrate every time this value changes instead of just on the fly changes.

A better solution would be writing custom [clean](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/instances/#django.db.models.Model.clean), or set [validators](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/forms/validation/#validators) attribute on a field.

Just jotting down what we discussed while it's fresh in my head.  We keep the essential structure of DirectoryWatcher - Load() spools events from the current _loader until it's exhausted, and then moves on to a new file.

However, we change the algorithm for moving on to a new file to do the following:

0. (setup) As state, we keep a "stream map" mapping from file suffixes to "stream entry" values that hold a loader instance, a timestamp, and a list of filenames
1. List the directory and get all filenames
2. Filter out any filenames that are "dead" (using a set of known "dead" files)
3. Find the next filename, per the following substeps:
3a. Order the filenames by timestamp, then by suffix.
3b. Seek ahead in the list to the first element with timestamp and suffix >= the current timestamp and suffix.
3c. Check if element.timestamp is >= to the timestamp value stored for element.suffix in the stream map.  If not, log an "out of order files" error and keep going until this is true.
3d. If this process failed to find a filename, null out the current timestamp and suffix and return.  (We'll resume with the next Load() call.)
4. Yield any remaining files from the current stream's loader (to handle sequence described [here](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/bf1e23229b8f9efbc5348ffbd7d9418c5fcb8b3c/tensorboard/backend/event_processing/directory_watcher.py#L121)).
5. If the last event to be yielded from the current stream's loader was > MAX_INACTIVE_AGE ago, add the current filename to the set of "dead" files, close the loader, and purge the current suffix's entry from the stream map
6. Set current timestamp and current suffix to those from the new filename
7. Look up the current suffix in the stream map
8. If there's an entry, check if the last file in list-of-files is the same as the new filename.  If so, we're done early and the routine ends here.
9. If there's an entry but the filename is different, do the following substeps:
9a. Close the loader in the stream entry, stat the stream entry's filename and record its size as the finalized size for that filename in a separate finalized_sizes map
9b. Add that filename to the set of dead files
9c. [Optional] check the last OOO_WRITE_CHECK_COUNT filenames in the list of files to see if they have out-of-order writes (meaning, they exist and their size != their finalized_sizes size) and if so log an error
10. If the current suffix didn't have a stream map entry, create one.
11. With the stream map entry:
11a. Update the timestamp to the current timestamp
11b. Append the new filename to the list-of-files
11c. Open a new loader for the current filename and set it as the stream map entry's loader

We're proposing that MAX_INACTIVE_AGE be set to 24 hours (or perhaps slightly more to allow for files written exactly once every 24 hours).  OOO_WRITE_CHECK_COUNT is currently 20 but I think it could be lower (like 1 or 2, really).

The goal of this new algorithm is that we still proceed with loading files by timestamp order, but once we get to the end of that order, we loop back to the beginning and re-read files again.  This handles the case where there are multiple active streams of data in different files.  To keep from having to re-read *every* file, we mark files as "dead" in two different ways: 1) if a file hasn't been written to in MAX_INACTIVE_AGE (as determined by last even time) and 2) if a file is preempted by a newer-timestamped file with the same suffix.  However, files that are dead of type 2 we still keep around to optionally check if they have been modified in case we want to detect out-of-order writes.  The end result should be that for a static set of files (e.g. a terminated experiment), we do read all the data, but that once we've gone through the initial set of files, we only reload data from an set of active "head" files of the streams that might still be open, which is one stream per suffix for files written to in the last 24 hours.  This keeps us from having to keep event file readers open and querying for new data for every single event file, even for very old logdirs.

@DuaneEllis-TI, separating the log output stream from the CLI is very useful.  OpenThread can actually be configured to use the platform-provided `otPlatLog()` implementation or to stream them through the CLI or Spinel.  See [`OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_ENABLE_DEFAULT_LOG_OUTPUT`](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/openthread-core-default-config.h#L618).  Having a separate log output stream on embedded platforms would be a great enhancement.

Note that in the posix example platform, [`otPlatLog()`](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/examples/platforms/posix/logging.c#L73) utilizes `syslog`, which enables a number of nice logging facilities in addition to separating the log output stream from the CLI.

An application should only use ML-EID addresses (RLOCs are for Thread internal use).
So if a (Thread) CoAP message is sent by an application it will have an ML-EID source address.
Since this is not allowed by Thread (section 10.6.2), there is an impasse. Either
- block those (as done in #1653), but this breaks functionality
- Replace ML-EID by RLOC

@librasungirl I disagree, the aim of the address resolution if to find an RLOC (This is an IP feature), having received an RLOC, allows you to use a short address on the MAC layer (call this a bonus). Said differently, since we cannot route (IP-layer) ML-EIDs, we have to switch to RLOCs. The fact that communication between routers uses 6lowpan meshing is irrelevant.

So, it is possible to get sheet names before consuming a lot of memory. but I do not recommend to use for now because internal api are subjected to change:

```
>>> from pyexcel.internal.core import get_book_stream
>>> ibook = get_book_stream(file_name="test-book.xlsx", on_demand=True)
>>> ibook.sheets
OrderedDict([(u'Sheet A', <pyexcel.internal.generators.SheetStream object at 0x0000000002E211D0>), (u'Sheet B', <pyexcel.internal.generators.SheetStream object at 0x00000000024EA320>), (u'Sheet C', <pyexcel.internal.generators.SheetStream object at 0x0000000002E44EB8>)])
>>> ibook.sheets.keys()
[u'Sheet A', u'Sheet B', u'Sheet C']
```

Please wait for official support for the access to internal book streams.

With that said, if you really want to save memory consumption or want a more efficient xlsx reader, you can consider pyexcel-xlsxr, which is under-used, zero stars at the moment. With above code, pyexcel-xlsxr returns faster than pyexcel-xlsx.

`self.get_connection()` decides if nodes are available only if there are retires left for that node i.e.

```python
        for node in self.retries:
```

and the next node is picked if the existing node has been put into delay i.e. 

```python
        if current_time_ms > picked_time:
```

Without a code example it can be difficult to work out exactly what's happening in your case. That said, my guess is that you're using raw `std::thread`. In that case, this isn't really a pybind11 issue, since this is a fundamental danger with `std::thread`: an unhandled exception will call `std::terminate`. You are expected to manually do all the exception handling when working with `std::thread` (it's the threading equivalent of a raw pointer). As a solution, you could try switching to `std::future` and `std::async` which do automatically handle the propagation of exceptions (the master thread which invokes `std::future::get()` will be expected to handle the exception).

The keep-alive in question is entirely internal to the pybind implementation.  Essentially what we do is created an internal python list and, if a temporary `array` is needed, we store a reference to it in that list, which keeps it (and more importantly, its data) alive.

The list itself is cleared when the function call ends, at which point destruction of the temporary is allowed to occur.  So essentially, when you call a bound function with a conforming type (i.e. matching dtype and compatible strides), the `Eigen::Ref` points directly into the input array data.  If you call the bound function with a non-conforming type, it'll work similarly to calling a wrapper like this:

```Python
def f_wrap(m):
    x = np.array(m, dtype='double', order='F')
    f(x)
f_wrap(myarray)
```
where the temporary `x` lives for the duration of the `f()` call.  (Of course we're not actually calling another function, and this works for an arbitrary number of arguments, etc., but the basic idea of keeping a reference alive for the duration of the call is the same).

Agree with @bmerry.  A `string_view` is really meant to be an in-transit type, not meant to be stored beyond the duration of a function call unless you have some external guarantee that the data it's referencing will stay valid for the life of the `string_view` (`string_view` does not provide any such guarantee itself).  In most cases in C++, the valid lifetime is just the duration of the function call that accepts the `string_view`.  You are seeing it sometimes "work" here because the string view is referencing invalid memory that, just by luck, hasn't been overwritten with something else yet.  The wrong output and the decoding failure are happening because it *has* been overwritten.

Changing your `variant` to hold a `std::string` rather than a `string_view` is probably what you want to do here.  You can still accept a `string_view` (a `std::string` can be constructed from one), and can still return it, but when returning it needs to be a `string_view` into a `string` that you own.

>Do you want me to test the function _resolve_conflict_via_command directly, after monkey-patching the execvp call?

I don't think you'd have to patch on such a low level. I was thinking of patching `subprocess.Popen` (assuming `check_call` uses that, otherwise patch `check_call` itself) using [pytest's monkeypatching support](http://doc.pytest.org/en/latest/monkeypatch.html)

So you'd need to add a function parameter `monkeypatch` to your test, which is then recognized by pytest as some sort of import, and then use the following line once:

    monkeypatch.setattr("subprocess.Popen", my_custom_popen)

Cleanup will happen automatically after the test, so calling `monkeypatch.undo()` or similar is not necessary.

You can just raise an exception from within there to avoid returning a useful value, since this is just for validating whether it has been correctly called. To catch that exception you can use [`pytest.raises`](http://doc.pytest.org/en/latest/assert.html#assertions-about-expected-exceptions) (a normal function you'd have to normally import).

> The secondary motivation for this is to address the devs who will inevitably use pip install -e . to set up their dev env, ignoring the documentation.

Those people also need to know about our OS dependencies though and because of this I unfortunately don't think Certbot is set up very well to support this use case.

> Tertiarily: people using certbot as a lib.

I believe this only affects them if they're using `pip install` which we agreed we're not worried about. Perhaps we should think differently about this case or offer a better way to install Certbot for people using it as a lib where it's not packaged though.

I think we should do something here, but I don't think that what we do should affect packaging. To build on what I said about OS packages before for @ohemorange's benefit, if we say we require `python-augeas<=0.5.0` and only a newer version is available, Certbot will immediately crash saying the right dependencies aren't available. If we don't pin, Certbot will at least try to work with the new dependency. I'm not aware of any client side Python projects that pin their dependencies like this. I asked Harlan how this would work in Debian and he said that a newer version of our dependency would be uploaded without being tested with a version of Certbot.

With this in mind, I don't think pinning helps OS packages and actually creates additional problems for them. Pinning also doesn't help any of our other distribution methods (that we said we cared about) so I don't think our solution here should affect packaging.

Proposal: Use the `letsencrypt-auto` requirements file to install deps in `venv.sh`, and the relevant `tox` tests. We can either modify the requirements file and/or the release process so the latest released version of Certbot isn't installed here or we can just install latest released version and overwrite it with the local packages. I'll also probably create a nightly test that tests with the latest packages so we can be aware of breakage as it occurs without slowing down development.

Thoughts?

This was a design decision made when writing the plugin. Currently, in addition to parsing any files included by Apache, we also parse the `vhost_root` as it is defined in [this file](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-apache/certbot_apache/constants.py) for your particular OS. This decision was explained a bit [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/2949#issuecomment-218992638), but just because your virtual host isn't enabled doesn't mean that Certbot can't help you set up HTTPS for that name.

My two cents: I think there's a balance to strike here. More information helps us solve the problem, but asking too many questions is likely to frustrate people and/or cause them to not fill out the form.

I have some suggestions on things to remove below, but I personally think asking for relevant Apache/Nginx configuration files is extremely valuable as most problems with Apache or Nginx need this information to be resolved.

> My domain is:

> I ran this command:

>  It produced this output: 

All of this information *should* be automatically included in Certbot's logs, but it might be awkward to leave some of these questions out as they're somewhat standard for these kind of forms. Perhaps we shouldn't ask for the domain directly and rely on the log?

> My hosting provider, if applicable, is:

> I'm using a control panel to manage my site (no, or provide the name and version of the control panel):

I'm not sure if these are necessary here as it's rare for your hosting provider/control panel to be relevant when resolving an issue.

> I can login to a root shell on my machine (yes or no, or I don't know):

`root` is also less of an issue. It has come up, but `certbot-auto` automatically tries to `sudo`/`su` to `root` (and refuses to work if that fails) and we have half way decent error messages if you try to run `certbot` itself without `root`:

```
If running as non-root, set --config-dir, --work-dir, and --logs-dir to writeable paths.
```

@bmw I am going to deposit my reasoning as well, but take it with a grain of salt because I am not aware of many things at this project:

As I understand, acme is a *protocol implementation*. With that being said, any deviation from the protocol should not be acceptable.

Therefore, to your points:

1) The client just invoked a functionality of the RFC and they expect the protocol implementation to act accordingly. If acme starts raising exceptions when they are not expected I am not sure how will the user will react.
2) The lowest common denominator are the *must*s as per RFC2119. Therefore, it is an error to expect something in the body of the request when the CA *may* return it there. The only guarantee is that it will be on the headers so this is where the client should look at.
3) I am not sure if the users should be considered at this point. Acme being a *protocol implementation* should be just that, and not consider the users. The users should be aware of the documentation and what outcomes their actions will have. All these questions should be answered by the RFC (and as I see in 7.3.2 they are) so therefore implementing that would be a solution.
4) And that is fine.

Also, I understand that people will use acme not only with Boulder but with other CAs as well, therefore we cannot depend on Boulder getting a fix.

Ok cool! So what's happening here is that the config file says `listen 443` instead of `listen 443 ssl`. But that's ok, this is totally valid for your nginx setup **because there is another server block listening on 443 over ssl**, which makes all server blocks listen on that port over ssl. Which is a behavior that we've recently realized exists. The fix for this should be in parsing vhosts, to post-process them to indicate sslishness based on this nginx behavior.

(For some background, the snakeoil inserting thing shouldn't be happening in the first place, because that happens in `_make_server_ssl`. Again, good that it's erroring out here.)

Also, we should maybe update the error message to be more helpful, like passing it up a few methods until we know which configuration method it came from rather than the low-level parser.

@ajaegers please file another ticket if you need help getting multiple Certbots running in parallel with `--config-dir`, `--work-dir` and `--logs-dir`. I think that should be a good solution for your use case; you probably want to seed the `--config-dir` directory by copying `/etc/letsencrypt` there, and then selectively deleting renewal config files (the ones in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/`) to ensure that each cert lives in exactly one home. You can also delete the corresponding subdirectories below the `live/` and `archive/` config subdirectories, but that's not strictly necessary.

The alternative would be supporting a `--unsafe-no-locking` option for cases like yours, with the understanding that you might have to fix things if you hit a race condition. Again, please file another bug if you need that!

I love the move to `function` and `class`. Getting rid of `actor` is actually a good thing, IMHO. The notion of a class was actually invented to encapsulate state -- it was the original OO motivation for it, so it's very fitting to call it a class semantically.

I also strongly support the unification of the calling interface for functions and methods with the `remote` suffix. The only tweak I would consider is getting rid of the `remote` suffix when instantiating the Ray Class. That way, *all* calls to `remote` can participate in the hybrid dataflow, return an object ID, and are easily identifiable in the code as places that trigger task submission to Ray. If, however, a call to `Class.remote()` can participate in dataflow and does return an object ID, it's fine to keep it as is. I think it's important that we have this futures composition marker well defined.

We're working on supporting GPU backed buffers in Plasma, but that is a work in progress.

> I am trying to play a downloaded stream but VLC doesn't launch after the stream downloads.

Streamlink doesn't launch any player process when using the `-o` parameter. If you're writing a stream to the filesystem, you have to open that file in your player afterwards.
https://streamlink.github.io/cli.html#cmdoption-o

> I have removed the # sign from : C:\Program Files (x86)\VideoLAN\VLC\vlc.exe in the config file at %APPDATA%\streamlink\streamlinkrc

This is redundant, as this exact path (default VLC installation path) will be used by default.
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/0.9.0/src/streamlink_cli/utils/player.py#L15-L31

> Is there a location of the output

If you don't specify a directory in the `-o` parameter (just a file name), then the current directory will be used, which in your case is `C:\Windows\system32`. The Streamlink installers on Windows add Streamlink's installation directory to your system's `PATH` environment variable, which means that you're able to call `streamlink` (or `streamlink.exe`, if you prefer that) from every directory of your system. So either `cd` into the intended directory or explicitly set it in the `-o` param like this `-o 'C:\foo\bar\baz.mp4'`.

I ran into this problem as well when trying to download something from the Swedish equivalent of this site. You'll notice that the working links all end in a numerical program id ('817236' in the working url above) while the non-working ones end in some textual description ('episode-5' in the one above). `streamlink` needs a numerical *program id* to be able to find the streams associated with the url, without this *id* it will complain as seen above. What you need to do is find the *program id* somewhere in the page referenced by the url. As *viafree* uses `react` the page source comprises of a chunk of JSON with an HTML header attached to it, making it harder to scrape than necessary. Fortunately you can get the data you need from the header as the *program id* is also used to get the thumbnail images. You can extract it from e.g. the 'og:image' `meta` tag using the following incantation:

```bash
id=$(xidel -s -e "//meta[@property='og:image']/@content" "$url" | cut -d '/' -f 9)
```

This uses [xidel](http://www.videlibri.de/xidel.html) (make sure to use the latest version which supports the *xpath* syntax and other parameters used in this example) to extract the contents of the relevant `meta` tag, the results of which are piped through `cut` to get at the correct path component. This might not be the most robust way to get at the data but for now it works. If you want to save the stream to a file you can do the same for the file name to which the data should be written:

```bash
filename=$(xidel -s -e "//meta[@property='og:title']/@content" "$url" | iconv -t ASCII//TRANSLIT|tr -d -c '[:alnum:][:blank:]'|tr -s '[:blank:]' _).mp4
```

Now you just need to modify the original url by adding the program id and streamlink will know how to handle it:

```bash
url=https://www.viafree.no/programmer/underholdning/paradise-hotel/sesong-9/episode-5
id=$(xidel -s -e "//meta[@property='og:image']/@content" "$url" | cut -d '/' -f 9)
url="${url}/${id}"
streamlink "$url"
```

Setting the log level via command line switch does this:

```
logging.getLogger('').setLevel(logging.getLevelName(value))
```

Thanks for posting the gist. Interestingly, when I run that code on my machine (Mac OS, `moto==1.0.1`), it fails with a moto-internal exception.

After a bit of debugging, it turns out that the bucket key in `put_object` should not be prefixed with a slash (`/`), see for instance here: http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/quickstart.html . In other words, if I change this:
```
        s3_client.put_object(
            Bucket='mybucket',
            Key='/foo.sym',
            Body=file_buffer,
            **extras,
        )
```
